Aluno
Travel English -- 48 Aulas

Ana Paula Porto de Oliveira Fornazari

Inglês do dia a dia — apresentar-se, rotina e conversação

B1 (Intermediário) Estados Unidos Médica (revalidando diploma) 60 min · Online
Progresso Geral0%
Velocidade:
Lesson 01 -- Pre-class

Who Is Ana Paula? -- Introducing Yourself in Everyday English

Introducing yourself in everyday English. Key words: doctor, routine, neighborhood, documentary, to improve, pronunciation, to practice, hobby, health, weekend, to describe, to study. Structure: present simple (routines, habits) vs present continuous (right now, this year).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Doctor -- a person who helps and treats people who are sick
"I am a doctor from Brazil."
Routine -- the things you do regularly, in the same order, every day
"My morning routine starts with coffee and English."
Neighborhood -- the area around your home, with its streets and people
"I love the quiet park in my neighborhood."
Documentary -- a film that tells true facts about real life, history or nature
"On weekends, I watch a documentary about history."
To improve -- to get better at something
"I practice every day to improve my English."
Pronunciation -- the way you say the sounds of words
"I want to improve my pronunciation."
To practice -- to do something many times to get better at it
"I practice speaking at dinners with friends."
Hobby -- an activity you do for fun in your free time
"My favorite hobby is walking my dogs."
Health -- the condition of your body and mind
"As a doctor, I care a lot about health."
Weekend -- Saturday and Sunday, when most people do not work
"On the weekend, I relax and see friends."
To describe -- to say what something or someone is like
"Can you describe your normal day?"
To study -- to learn something by reading, practicing or going to classes
"Right now, I am studying for my medical exams."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Doctor
Routine
Neighborhood
Documentary
To improve
Pronunciation
To practice
Hobby
Health
Weekend
To describe
To study

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Ana Paula is a doctor from Brazil, and she lives in the United States now. Every morning, she studies English before breakfast, because she wants to improve her pronunciation. This year, she is studying for her medical exams and is revalidating her diploma. She has a busy routine, but she loves her new neighborhood. On weekends, she watches documentaries about history and walks her two dogs in the park. Right now, she is planning a small dinner to practice English with her neighbors.

1. Why do we say "Right now, she is studying for her exams"?
A Because it is happening around now (present continuous).
B Because it is a habit she does every day.
C Because it finished a long time ago.
2. "Every morning, she studies English" uses the present simple because:
A It is happening only at this moment.
B It is a routine she does regularly.
C It is a plan for next year.
3. Which sentence about her hobby is correct?
A "She watch documentaries on weekends."
B "She watches documentaries on weekends."
C "She is watch documentaries on weekends."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Simple vs Present Continuous

GRAMMAR

How to talk about your everyday life with the right tense.

FormUseExample
Present Simple
I / you / we + verb (he/she + s)		Routines, habits and permanent facts (every day, usually, always).I study English every day.
Present Continuous
am / is / are + verb-ing		Actions now or around now, temporary situations (right now, this year, at the moment).Right now, I am studying.
he / she / itIn the present simple, add -s to the verb for he, she or it.She watches a documentary.
Stative verbs
know, like, want, need, have		Verbs of feeling and state are not used in the continuous. Say I know, not "I am knowing".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"Right now, I for my medical exams."
"Every morning, I my dogs in the park."
"This year, I my diploma."
"She a documentary every weekend."
"I the answer."
"At the moment, the neighbors a party."

Stage 2: Put the Conversation in Order

Order

Put the steps of a dinner conversation in the correct order.

?Talk about your daily routine and what you are doing now.
?Say it was nice to talk and agree to meet again.
?Say hello and introduce yourself with your name.
?Ask the other person a question about their life.
?Say where you are from and what you do.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

Hi, I am Ana Paula. Nice to meet you.
I am a doctor from Brazil, and I live here now.
Right now, I am studying to work as a doctor here.
Every morning, I study English and walk my dogs.
On weekends, I watch documentaries and relax.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real situation.

A guest asks: "So, what do you do?" You answer:
A "I do doctor things in a hospital."
B "I am a doctor from Brazil. Right now, I am studying to work here."
C "I am study to be a doctor since one year."
Someone asks about your routine. The best answer is:
A "Right now, I walk my dogs every morning."
B "Every morning, I walk my dogs and study English."
C "Every morning, I am walking my dogs and study English."
You want to talk about a project this year. Which is correct?
A "This year, I am revalidating my medical diploma."
B "This year, I revalidate my medical diploma."
C "This year, I am revalidate my medical diploma."
A friend asks if you understand. You feel sure and say:
A "Yes, I know the answer."
B "Yes, I am knowing the answer."
C "Yes, I am know the answer."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are at a dinner and a new person asks: "So, who are you? Tell me about your normal day." Introduce yourself: your job (present simple), your routine (present simple), and what you are doing this year (present continuous). Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 1

1Hi, I am Ana Paula. Nice to meet you.
2I am a doctor from Brazil, and I live here now.
3Right now, I am studying to work as a doctor here.
4On weekends, I walk my dogs and watch documentaries.
5It was great to talk. Let us meet again soon.
Lesson 02 -- Pre-class

My Everyday Life -- Routines & Free Time

Describing your routine and free time. Key words: errand, chore, commute, workout, leisure, to unwind, groceries, nap, stroll, habit, to catch up, schedule. Grammar: adverbs of frequency (always, usually, often, sometimes, rarely, never) and where they go in the sentence.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Errand -- a quick trip to do a small task, like the bank or the pharmacy
"I usually run one or two errands after work."
Chore -- a small job you do at home, such as cleaning or the dishes
"On Sunday I do the chores around the house."
Commute -- the regular trip between your home and your workplace
"My commute takes about thirty minutes each way."
Workout -- a period of physical exercise, like running or the gym
"I do a short workout three times a week."
Leisure -- free time when you rest and do the things you enjoy
"I keep my Sundays for leisure and family."
To unwind -- to relax and let go of stress after a busy day
"After work, I like to unwind with a walk."
Groceries -- the food and everyday items you buy at the supermarket
"I buy my groceries once a week."
Nap -- a short sleep, usually taken during the day
"On weekends I sometimes take a short nap."
Stroll -- a slow, relaxed walk that you take for pleasure
"We often take an evening stroll after dinner."
Habit -- something you do regularly, often without thinking about it
"Reading before bed is a good habit."
To catch up -- to reconnect with someone, or finish something you were behind on
"On Saturday I catch up with old friends."
Schedule -- a plan that shows the times when you do each activity
"I keep a simple schedule during the week."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Errand
Chore
Commute
Workout
Leisure
To unwind
Groceries
Nap
Stroll
Habit
To catch up
Schedule

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Ana Paula has a busy but calm schedule. She always drinks a coffee before her commute, which usually takes about thirty minutes. She often does a short workout in the morning, but she rarely takes a nap during the day. After work, she sometimes runs a few errands, and then she likes to unwind. In the evening, she often takes a slow stroll in her neighborhood. On the weekend, she never works. She catches up with friends, does the groceries, and finishes the chores at home. A relaxed evening is her favorite habit.

1. Why do we say "She always drinks coffee" and not "She drinks always coffee"?
A The adverb of frequency goes before the main verb.
B "Always" must go at the end of the sentence.
C "Always" is a noun, so it goes after the verb.
2. How often does Ana Paula work on the weekend?
A She always works on the weekend.
B She never works on the weekend.
C She usually works on the weekend.
3. Which sentence puts the adverb in the correct place with the verb "to be"?
A "She never is tired after work."
B "She is never tired after work."
C "She is tired never after work."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Adverbs of Frequency

GRAMMAR

How to say how often you do things, and where the adverb goes.

RuleDetailExample
The scalealways (100%) > usually > often > sometimes > rarely > never (0%).I usually walk to work.
Main verbsThe adverb goes BEFORE the main verb.I always drink coffee.
The verb "to be"With am / is / are, the adverb goes AFTER the verb.She is never late.
Time expressionsPhrases like every day, once a week or on weekends usually go at the END of the sentence.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct adverb of frequency. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"I drink coffee in the morning."
"She takes the bus; she prefers to walk."
"We go to the movies on a Friday."
"He is tired after his long commute."
"I do the groceries on Saturday."
"They eat out during the week."

Stage 2: Put the Routine in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a normal day in the correct order.

?My commute often takes about thirty minutes.
?On the weekend, I catch up with friends and do the groceries.
?In the morning, I always drink my coffee first.
?In the evening, I sometimes go for a stroll to unwind.
?I usually go for a short workout before work.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

I always drink coffee before my commute.
She is never late for work.
I sometimes take a short nap on the weekend.
After work, I often go for a stroll to unwind.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real situation.

A friend asks: "How often do you exercise?" You answer:
A "I workout, yes."
B "I usually work out three times a week."
C "I am work out always."
You describe a daily habit. Which is correct?
A "I always check my schedule in the morning."
B "I check always my schedule in the morning."
C "Always I check my schedule in the morning."
You talk about something you dislike. Which is correct?
A "I rarely enjoy doing the chores."
B "I enjoy rarely doing the chores."
C "Rarely I enjoy the chores doing."
You say how often you feel tired. Which is correct?
A "I am often tired at night."
B "I often am tired at night."
C "I tired am often at night."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

A friend asks about a normal day in your life. Describe your routine and your free time, from morning to evening. Talk about your commute, your workout, your chores and errands, and how you unwind. Use at least four adverbs of frequency, and put each one in the right place. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 2

1I usually wake up early and go for a workout.
2My commute always takes about thirty minutes.
3I sometimes take a nap on the weekend.
4After work, I often go for a stroll to unwind.
5I never skip my morning coffee.
Lesson 03 -- Pre-class

My Neighborhood & My City

Describing your neighborhood and city. Key words: avenue, sidewalk, corner, bakery, pharmacy, square, landmark, crosswalk, traffic, block, convenient, downtown. Structure: there is / there are with prepositions of place (next to, across from, between, behind, in front of, on the corner of, near).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Avenue -- a wide street in a city, often lined with trees
"There is a busy avenue near my apartment."
Sidewalk -- the paved path at the side of a street where people walk
"The sidewalks here are wide and clean."
Corner -- the point where two streets meet
"There is a coffee shop on the corner."
Bakery -- a shop that makes and sells bread and cakes
"There is a small bakery across from my building."
Pharmacy -- a shop where you buy medicine
"The pharmacy is next to the supermarket."
Square -- an open public space in a city, surrounded by buildings
"There is a beautiful square in the center of town."
Landmark -- a famous building or place that is easy to recognize
"The old church is the main landmark in my neighborhood."
Crosswalk -- the marked place where people cross the street
"Always use the crosswalk to cross the avenue."
Traffic -- the cars and vehicles moving on the roads
"There is a lot of traffic in the morning."
Block -- a group of buildings surrounded by four streets
"The gym is two blocks from my house."
Convenient -- easy to reach and close to what you need
"My neighborhood is very convenient for shopping."
Downtown -- the central business area of a city
"I work downtown, near the main square."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Avenue
Sidewalk
Corner
Bakery
Pharmacy
Square
Landmark
Crosswalk
Traffic
Block
Convenient
Downtown

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Ana Paula lives on a quiet avenue downtown, and she loves her new neighborhood. There is a small bakery on the corner, right across from her building, and there are two pharmacies near the square. The old church is the main landmark, and there is a lovely park behind it. Everything is very convenient: there are shops, a market and a gym only two blocks away. In the morning there is a lot of traffic on the avenue, so she always uses the crosswalk to cross safely. On weekends, she takes a stroll along the quiet sidewalks.

1. Why do we say "There is a small bakery" and not "There are"?
A Because it is one place (singular).
B Because it is more than one place (plural).
C Because the bakery is far away.
2. "There are two pharmacies" uses "are" because:
A There is only one pharmacy.
B There is more than one pharmacy (plural).
C The pharmacies are closed.
3. Which sentence about the park is correct?
A "Have a park behind the church."
B "There is a park behind the church."
C "There is a park in front the church."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- There is / There are + Prepositions of Place

GRAMMAR

How to say what exists in a place and where it is.

FormUseExample
There is
(There's)		One thing -- singular. Say what exists (never "have").There is a bakery on the corner.
There areTwo or more things -- plural.There are two parks near my home.
Negative & questionThere is not / There are not; Is there...? / Are there...?Is there a pharmacy near here?
Prepositions of placeSay WHERE it is: next to, across from, between, behind, in front of, on the corner of, near. (Never drop the "of": in front of.)

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"There a bakery on the corner of my street."
"There three shops near my home."
"The pharmacy is the market."
"My building is the square."
"There a lot of traffic in the morning."
"There is a small park the church."

Stage 2: Put the Description in Order

Order

Put the steps of describing your neighborhood in the correct order. Tap Listen to hear the full description.

?Describe the shops and places near your home.
?Invite the guest to take a stroll around the block.
?Welcome the guest and say where you live.
?Explain why the location is so convenient.
?Say what there is on your street or corner.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

There is a bakery on the corner of my street.
There are many shops near my building.
The pharmacy is next to the supermarket.
My neighborhood is very convenient.
Let us take a stroll around the block.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real situation.

A visitor asks: "Is there a bakery near here?" You answer:
A "Yes, have a bakery on the corner."
B "Yes, there is one on the corner, across from my building."
C "Yes, there are a bakery on the corner."
You want to say your area has many shops. The best answer is:
A "There is many shops downtown."
B "There are many shops downtown."
C "Have many shops downtown."
You give directions to the pharmacy. Which is correct?
A "It is next to the market, in front of the square."
B "It is next the market, in front the square."
C "It is next to the market, in front square."
A friend asks why you like your area. You say:
A "It is very convenient. There are shops and a park near my building."
B "It is very convenient. There is shops and a park near my building."
C "It is very convenient. Have shops and a park near my building."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

A new friend asks: "Tell me about your neighborhood." Describe it: say what there is (there is / there are), where the main places are (use prepositions of place), and why it is convenient. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 3

1There is a bakery on the corner of my street.
2There are many shops near my building.
3The pharmacy is next to the supermarket.
4My neighborhood is very convenient.
5Let us take a stroll around the block.
Lesson 04 -- Pre-class

Talking About the Past -- My Story

Telling your own story and trajectory. Key words: childhood, to grow up, background, to graduate, career, to relocate, milestone, turning point, to achieve, challenge, to overcome, memory. Grammar: the past simple -- regular and irregular verbs, and negatives and questions with did.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Childhood -- the time in your life when you were a child
"My childhood was happy and full of good memories."
To grow up -- to change from a child into an adult over the years
"I grew up in a small town in the countryside."
Background -- your education, family history, and past experience
"She has a strong background in science."
To graduate -- to finish your studies and receive a diploma
"I graduated from university in 2010."
Career -- the series of jobs you have over the years in one field
"He built a long career in medicine."
To relocate -- to move to a new city or country to live or work
"We relocated to another country for my job."
Milestone -- an important event or stage in your life
"Buying our first home was a real milestone."
Turning point -- a moment when an important change begins
"That trip was a turning point in my life."
To achieve -- to succeed in doing something after a lot of effort
"She achieved her dream of becoming a doctor."
Challenge -- a difficult task that tests your ability
"The new job was a big challenge for me."
To overcome -- to succeed in dealing with a problem or difficulty
"He overcame many problems to get here."
Memory -- something from the past that you remember
"That day is my favorite memory."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Childhood
To grow up
Background
To graduate
Career
To relocate
Milestone
Turning point
To achieve
Challenge
To overcome
Memory

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Ana Paula has an inspiring story. She grew up in a small town, and her childhood was simple and happy. Her family valued education, and that background shaped her dreams. She studied hard and graduated from medical school with pride. Her first years as a doctor were a real challenge, but she overcame every difficulty and built a strong career. The real turning point came when she decided to relocate to the United States. It was a huge milestone. The first months were hard, but she achieved her goal step by step. Today, that brave decision is her proudest memory.

1. Why do we say "She grew up" and not "She growed up"?
A "Grow" is an irregular verb, so its past form is "grew".
B "Grow" never changes in the past.
C "Grew" is the plural of "grow".
2. Which sentence is in the correct past simple form?
A "She decided to relocate to the United States."
B "She decide to relocate to the United States."
C "She did decided to relocate to the United States."
3. How do we make the negative of "She studied medicine"?
A "She did not study medicine."
B "She did not studied medicine."
C "She not studied medicine."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Past Simple

GRAMMAR

How to talk about finished actions in the past.

RuleDetailExample
Regular verbsAdd -ed to the base verb.I work → I worked.
Irregular verbsThe form changes; you learn each one.I grow up → I grew up.
NegativesUse did not (didn't) + the base verb.She did not study law.
QuestionsUse did + subject + the base verb: Did you graduate in 2010?

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct past simple verb. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"I up in a small town near the coast."
"She from university in 2015."
"My first job a real challenge."
"We to another city for work."
"He a doctor after many years."
"I up, even when it was hard."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a life story in the correct order.

?My first job was a challenge, but I overcame it.
?In the end, I achieved my dream.
?When I was a child, I grew up in a small town.
?A few years later, I relocated to a new city.
?I studied hard and graduated from university.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

I grew up in a small town in the countryside.
My first job was a real challenge.
I overcame many difficulties and built my career.
The day I graduated is my favorite memory.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real situation.

A colleague asks: "Where did you grow up?" You answer:
A "I grew up in a small town in the south."
B "I grow up in a small town in the south."
C "I am grow up in the south."
You talk about your studies. Which is correct?
A "I graduated from medical school in 2010."
B "I graduate from medical school in 2010."
C "I did graduated from medical school in 2010."
You describe a difficulty in the past. Which is correct?
A "The first year was hard, but I overcame it."
B "The first year was hard, but I overcomed it."
C "The first year is hard, but I overcame it."
Someone asks about your past. Which question is correct?
A "Did you enjoy your first job?"
B "Did you enjoyed your first job?"
C "You did enjoy your first job?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

A new friend asks about your life story. Tell them where you grew up, what you studied, your first job, and an important turning point or milestone. Use the past simple -- both regular verbs (worked, studied, moved) and irregular verbs (grew, was, became). Take your time and tell your story.

Survival Card -- Lesson 4

1I grew up in a small town.
2I studied hard and started my career.
3My first job was a real challenge.
4I overcame every difficulty.
5Relocating here is my proudest memory.
Lesson 05 -- Pre-class

A Memorable Trip

Narrating a memorable trip. Key words: journey, scenery, adventure, sightseeing, souvenir, breathtaking, landscape, to wander, coast, sunset, unforgettable, to get lost. Structure: past continuous vs past simple (was/were + -ing for the background action, past simple for the sudden moment; while + long action, when + short action).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Journey -- a trip from one place to another, usually a long one
"The journey to the mountains took six hours."
Scenery -- the natural features of an area, such as mountains and rivers
"The scenery along the coast was amazing."
Adventure -- an exciting or unusual experience, often with some risk
"Getting lost in the old town was a real adventure."
Sightseeing -- the activity of visiting interesting places as a tourist
"We spent the whole day sightseeing in the city."
Souvenir -- an object you buy to remember a place you visited
"I bought a small souvenir for my mother."
Breathtaking -- extremely beautiful or impressive
"The view from the top was breathtaking."
Landscape -- everything you can see when you look across a wide area of land
"The green landscape stretched to the horizon."
To wander -- to walk around slowly with no clear direction or plan
"We wandered through the narrow streets for hours."
Coast -- the land right next to the sea
"We drove along the coast at sunrise."
Sunset -- the time in the evening when the sun goes down
"We watched the sunset over the water."
Unforgettable -- so good or special that you can never forget it
"It was an unforgettable trip."
To get lost -- to lose your way and not know where you are
"We got lost in the forest before sunset."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Journey
Scenery
Adventure
Sightseeing
Souvenir
Breathtaking
Landscape
To wander
Coast
Sunset
Unforgettable
To get lost

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Last summer, Ana Paula went on the best journey of her life. One morning, while she was driving along the coast, the sun rose over the sea and the scenery was breathtaking. She stopped in a small town and wandered through the streets. While she was doing some sightseeing, it started to rain, so she bought a lovely souvenir and waited in a cafe. Later, she got lost for a while, but it became a real adventure. That evening, while everyone was watching the sunset, she took her favorite photo. It was an unforgettable day.

1. Why do we say "she was driving" and not "she drove"?
A It is the long background action in progress.
B It is a short, finished action.
C Driving never uses the past simple.
2. In "while she was doing some sightseeing, it started to rain", which action was sudden?
A The sightseeing (was doing).
B The rain starting (it started).
C Both happened slowly.
3. Which sentence about the sunset is correct?
A "While everyone watched the sunset, she was taking her photo."
B "While everyone was watching the sunset, she took her photo."
C "While everyone was watching the sunset, she was took her photo."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Past Continuous vs Past Simple

GRAMMAR

How to tell what was happening in the background and what happened in the moment.

FormUseExample
Past continuous
(was / were + -ing)		A longer action in progress -- the background of the story.I was walking on the beach.
Past simpleA short, finished action -- the moment that happened.I saw a whale.
Negative & questionwas not / were not + -ing; Was / Were + subject + -ing?Were you sleeping when I called?
when & whileUse while with the long action (was doing) and when with the short one (did): "While I was taking photos, I met a friend."

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"I on the beach when I saw a whale."
"While we , the sun rose over the coast."
"It started to rain while I a souvenir."
"When the storm stopped, we to the beach."
" I was doing some sightseeing, it started to rain."
"They were wandering through the town they got lost."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Put the steps of telling a memorable trip in the correct order. Tap Listen to hear the full description.

?Tell what happened suddenly.
?Say why the trip was unforgettable.
?Say where and when you traveled.
?Explain how you felt in that moment.
?Describe the scenery and what you were doing.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

Last year, I traveled to the coast.
While we were driving, the scenery was breathtaking.
Suddenly, it started to rain.
We got lost, but it was a real adventure.
It was an unforgettable trip.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real situation.

A friend asks what you were doing when the storm started. You answer:
A "I walked on the beach when the storm started."
B "I was walking on the beach when the storm started."
C "I was walk on the beach when the storm started."
You want to describe two friends hiking. The best sentence is:
A "We was hiking when we found the lake."
B "We were hiking when we found the lake."
C "We were hike when we found the lake."
You tell a story with "while". Which is correct?
A "While I was taking photos, I met a traveler."
B "While I took photos, I was meeting a traveler."
C "While I take photos, I met a traveler."
A friend asks why the trip was special. You say:
A "While we were watching the sunset, everything felt perfect. It was unforgettable."
B "While we watched the sunset, everything was felt perfect."
C "While we watching the sunset, everything felt perfect."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

A new friend asks: "Tell me about a memorable trip." Tell the story: say where and when you traveled, what was happening around you (past continuous), what happened suddenly (past simple), and why it was unforgettable. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 5

1Last year, I traveled to the coast.
2While we were driving, the scenery was breathtaking.
3Suddenly, it started to rain.
4We got lost, but it was a real adventure.
5It was an unforgettable trip.
Lesson 06 -- Pre-class

Describing People & Places

Describing people and places in vivid detail. Key words: outgoing, generous, reliable, easygoing, ambitious, cheerful, stubborn, cozy, spacious, crowded, lively, charming. Grammar: adjective order -- opinion, size, age, color, origin, then the noun.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Outgoing -- friendly and confident, and enjoys meeting new people
"My sister is very outgoing and makes friends everywhere."
Generous -- happy to give money, time, or help to others
"He is generous and always shares what he has."
Reliable -- someone you can trust to do what they promise
"She is reliable, so I can always count on her."
Easygoing -- relaxed and calm, and not easily upset
"My father is easygoing and never gets stressed."
Ambitious -- having a strong wish to succeed or achieve a lot
"She is ambitious and wants to open her own clinic."
Cheerful -- happy and positive in a way that people can see
"He is cheerful and always has a smile."
Stubborn -- not willing to change your mind, even when you are wrong
"My uncle is stubborn and never admits he is wrong."
Cozy -- small, warm, and comfortable in a pleasant way
"Their living room is small but very cozy."
Spacious -- with a lot of open space inside
"The new house is bright and spacious."
Crowded -- full of people, with little free space
"The beach is always crowded in summer."
Lively -- full of energy, activity, and excitement
"The market is lively and full of music."
Charming -- pleasant and attractive in a way that people like
"It is a charming old town by the river."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Outgoing
Generous
Reliable
Easygoing
Ambitious
Cheerful
Stubborn
Cozy
Spacious
Crowded
Lively
Charming

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

My aunt Rosa is my favorite person. She is a generous and easygoing woman who loves everyone. She runs a cozy little cafe on the corner, and it is the most charming place in our neighborhood. It is not spacious, but it feels warm and friendly. In the morning it is quiet, but by lunchtime it becomes lively and crowded. When friends ask about her, I always say the same thing: she is a warm, generous old lady with a beautiful little cafe.

1. Why do we say "a cozy little cafe" and not "a little cozy cafe"?
A Opinion adjectives (cozy) come before size adjectives (little).
B Size adjectives always come first in English.
C The order of adjectives does not matter in English.
2. Which phrase has the correct adjective order?
A "a beautiful little cafe"
B "a little beautiful cafe"
C "a cafe beautiful little"
3. Where do adjectives usually go in English?
A Before the noun: "a charming place."
B After the noun: "a place charming."
C Either before or after, it is the same.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Adjective Order

GRAMMAR

How to put two or more adjectives in the right order.

RuleDetailExample
Before the nounAdjectives come BEFORE the noun.a cozy room (not "a room cozy")
The orderopinion → size → age → color → origina lovely small old house
Two adjectivesJoin with "and" or a comma.She is kind and funny.
Same typeUse a comma between adjectives of the same type: a warm, generous woman.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct adjective. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"My grandmother is a very woman who loves to give."
"The little cafe is warm and ."
"He is and enjoys talking to new people."
"You can always trust her; she is completely ."
"On Saturday the market gets very ."
"Their new home is bright and ."

Stage 2: Put the Description in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the sentences of the description in the correct order.

?She runs a cozy little cafe on the corner.
?Everyone loves her charming smile.
?My favorite person is my aunt Rosa.
?In the evening, the cafe gets lively and crowded.
?She is a generous and easygoing woman.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

My best friend is generous, cheerful, and very easygoing.
She lives in a cozy little house near the park.
The old square gets lively and crowded on weekends.
He is a reliable and outgoing young man.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real situation.

A friend asks: "What is your sister like?" You answer:
A "She is outgoing, generous, and very cheerful."
B "She is outgoing generous cheerful."
C "She has outgoing and generous."
You describe your home. Which phrase is correct?
A "It is a cozy little apartment."
B "It is a little cozy apartment."
C "It is an apartment cozy little."
You want to say your friend can be trusted. You say:
A "He is very reliable; he never lets me down."
B "He is very crowded; he never lets me down."
C "He is very spacious; he never lets me down."
You describe a busy weekend market. Which is correct?
A "The market is lively and crowded on Saturdays."
B "The market is reliable and stubborn on Saturdays."
C "The market is easygoing and generous on Saturdays."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Describe a person you love and a place you love. For the person, use at least three adjectives for personality (outgoing, generous, reliable, easygoing, cheerful...). For the place, describe it too (cozy, spacious, lively, charming...). Remember the adjective order: opinion, size, age, color, origin, then the noun. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 6

1My best friend is very outgoing and generous.
2She is easygoing and always cheerful.
3It is a cozy little place on a quiet street.
4The square gets lively and crowded on weekends.
5He is a reliable, charming young man.
Lesson 07 -- Pre-class

Comparing Things

Comparing places, habits, and options. Key words: affordable, peaceful, modern, reasonable, efficient, similar, comfortable, option, advantage, drawback, to compare, to prefer. Structure: comparatives and superlatives (short adjectives with -er / -est, long adjectives with more / the most, irregular good-better-best and bad-worse-worst, and as ... as for equal things).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Affordable -- cheap enough for most people to buy or pay for
"This apartment is more affordable than the other one."
Peaceful -- calm and quiet, without noise or trouble
"The countryside is more peaceful than the city center."
Modern -- new and using the latest style or ideas
"Her kitchen is more modern than mine."
Reasonable -- fair and sensible, especially about a price
"The rent here is very reasonable for the area."
Efficient -- working well without wasting time or energy
"The subway is a more efficient way to travel than the bus."
Similar -- almost the same as something else
"The two neighborhoods are quite similar."
Comfortable -- giving a pleasant and relaxed feeling
"This sofa is more comfortable than that old chair."
Option -- one of the things you can choose
"Renting is a good option for a small family."
Advantage -- a good point that helps you or makes something better
"One advantage of this area is the quiet at night."
Drawback -- a disadvantage or a small problem with something
"The main drawback is the long trip to work."
To compare -- to look at two things to see how they are the same or different
"Let us compare the two apartments before we decide."
To prefer -- to like one thing more than another
"I prefer the quieter neighborhood."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Affordable
Peaceful
Modern
Reasonable
Efficient
Similar
Comfortable
Option
Advantage
Drawback
To compare
To prefer

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Ana Paula wants to move, so she decided to compare two apartments. The first one is more affordable than the second, and the rent is very reasonable. It is also in a more peaceful neighborhood, which is a big advantage for her. The second apartment is bigger and more modern, but it is also more expensive and less comfortable. The two kitchens are quite similar, but the first building has a more efficient elevator. The main drawback of the first apartment is the long trip to the hospital. In the end, she thinks the first one is the best option, because she prefers a quiet home to a fancy one.

1. Why do we say "more affordable" and not "affordabler"?
A Long adjectives use "more" instead of "-er".
B "Affordable" has no comparative form.
C "More" is only used with nouns.
2. In "the first one is the best option", why "the best"?
A It compares only two things.
B It is the superlative of "good", and it is the top of the group.
C "Best" is the comparative of "good".
3. Which sentence compares the two apartments correctly?
A "The second apartment is more bigger than the first."
B "The second apartment is bigger than the first."
C "The second apartment is the bigger than the first."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Comparatives and Superlatives

GRAMMAR

How to compare two things (comparative) and point to the top of a group (superlative).

TypeComparative (2 things)Superlative (the top)
Short adjective
(cheap, quiet)		adjective + -er + than
cheaper than, quieter than		the + adjective + -est
the cheapest, the quietest
Long adjective
(modern, affordable)		more + adjective + than
more modern than		the most + adjective
the most modern
Irregulargood → better than; bad → worse thangood → the best; bad → the worst
EqualUse as ... as when two things are the same: "This apartment is as modern as that one."

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"This apartment is than the other one."
"The subway is than the bus."
"For me, this is the option of all."
"The countryside is than the city."
"Her apartment is the in the building."
"This sofa is the old one."

Stage 2: Put the Comparison in Order

Order

Put the steps of comparing two options in the correct order. Tap Listen to hear the full description.

?Compare another feature, like the size or the location.
?Say which one you prefer and give your final choice.
?Name the two options you want to compare.
?Say which option is the best and explain why.
?Compare their price with a comparative like cheaper or more expensive.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

This apartment is cheaper than the other one.
The park is the most peaceful place in the city.
I prefer the more modern kitchen.
Renting is a better option for us right now.
The two neighborhoods are quite similar.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real situation.

A friend asks you to compare two cafes. You say:
A "This cafe is more cheaper than that one."
B "This cafe is cheaper than that one."
C "This cafe is the cheaper than that one."
You want to say one gym is number one of all. You say:
A "This is the most modern gym in the area."
B "This is the more modern gym in the area."
C "This is most modern gym in the area."
You compare working from home and going to the office. Which is correct?
A "Working from home is gooder than the office."
B "Working from home is better than going to the office."
C "Working from home is more better than the office."
You want to say two options are the same. You say:
A "The first plan is as reasonable as the second one."
B "The first plan is as reasonable than the second one."
C "The first plan is so reasonable as the second one."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

A friend asks you to help them choose between two neighborhoods, two apartments, or two ways to travel to work. Compare the two options: say which one is more affordable, which one has a better location, and which one you prefer and why. Use comparatives and superlatives. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 7

1This apartment is more affordable than the other one.
2The subway is more efficient than the bus.
3I think this is the best option for us.
4The main drawback is the long trip to work.
5I prefer the quieter and more peaceful neighborhood.
Lesson 08 -- Pre-class

Midpoint Review -- How Far Have I Come?

A midpoint review of Block 1. Key words: journey, milestone, turning point, achieve, overcome, challenge, improve, routine, neighborhood, generous, unforgettable, habit. Grammar review: present simple for routines, past simple for finished actions, comparatives for progress.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These words came up across Lessons 1 to 7. Listen to each one and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Journey -- a long process of learning or change over time
"Learning English has been a long journey for me."
Milestone -- an important moment or achievement on the way
"My first full conversation was a big milestone."
Turning point -- a moment when an important change begins
"That class was a real turning point for me."
To achieve -- to succeed in doing something after effort
"I want to achieve fluency in English."
To overcome -- to succeed in dealing with a difficult problem
"She worked hard to overcome her fear of speaking."
Challenge -- something difficult that tests your ability
"Understanding fast speech is still a challenge."
To improve -- to get better at something
"My pronunciation has improved a lot this month."
Routine -- the things you do regularly, in the same order
"Studying every morning is part of my routine."
Neighborhood -- the area around your home
"I can now describe my neighborhood in English."
Generous -- happy to give time, help, or money to others
"My teacher is generous with her feedback."
Unforgettable -- so special that you cannot forget it
"My first full conversation was unforgettable."
Habit -- something you do often, almost without thinking
"Reading in English is now a daily habit."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Journey
Milestone
Turning point
To achieve
To overcome
Challenge
To improve
Routine
Neighborhood
Generous
Unforgettable
Habit

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

A few months ago, I decided to change my life. English was a real challenge, and my old routine had no time for study. So I built a new habit: now I study every morning before work. My first full conversation was an unforgettable milestone. Slowly, I began to overcome my fear. Today my English is much better than before, and I am more confident than I was at the start.

1. Which sentence correctly describes a daily routine (present simple)?
A "I study every morning before work."
B "I studied every morning now."
C "I am study every morning."
2. Which sentence is in the past simple?
A "My first conversation was unforgettable."
B "My English is better now."
C "I study every day."
3. Which sentence correctly shows progress (comparative)?
A "My English is better than before."
B "My English is more good than before."
C "My English is gooder than before."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Tenses of Block 1

GRAMMAR

Bring the three main tenses of Block 1 together in one story.

TenseUse it forExample
Present simpleroutines and habits (now)I study every morning.
Past simplefinished actions in the pastI started, I learned.
Comparativesprogress and changeMy English is better than before.
Irregular formsSome forms are irregular: go → went, good → better (never "more good").

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"Every morning I English before work."
"Last year I to learn English again."
"My pronunciation is than before."
"My first conversation was a real ."
"I worked hard to my fear of speaking."
"Studying every day is now a good ."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the sentences of the story in the correct order.

?My first full conversation was an unforgettable milestone.
?Learning English has been a long journey.
?Now my English is much better than before.
?At first, speaking was a real challenge.
?Then I changed my daily routine and studied every morning.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

Learning English has been a long journey for me.
At first it was a challenge, but I did not give up.
My English is much better than before.
I still have a lot to achieve, and I am ready.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real situation.

A friend asks: "What was hard at the start?" You answer:
A "At first, speaking was a real challenge."
B "At first, speaking is a real challenge."
C "At first, speaking was a real journey."
You want to describe your progress. Which is correct?
A "My English is better than before."
B "My English is more good than before."
C "My English is more better than before."
You talk about your daily study. Which is correct?
A "I study English every morning."
B "I studied English every morning now."
C "I am study English every morning."
You describe an important moment in your journey. You say:
A "My first conversation was an unforgettable milestone."
B "My first conversation was an unforgettable routine."
C "My first conversation was an unforgettable neighborhood."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Look back at your English journey so far. Where did you start? What was the biggest challenge? What milestone are you most proud of? How has your English improved? Mix the past simple (I started, I learned), the present simple (I study, I practice), and comparatives (better, more confident). Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 8

1Learning English has been a long journey for me.
2At first it was a real challenge, but I did not give up.
3My English is much better than before.
4My first conversation was an unforgettable milestone.
5I still have a lot to achieve, and I am ready.
Lesson 09 -- Pre-class

Health & Wellbeing

Talking about health, energy, and wellbeing. Key words: wellbeing, balanced, exhausted, stress, symptom, checkup, to recover, fit, mindful, to avoid, to cut down on, to cope with. Structure: modals of advice -- should and shouldn't for friendly suggestions, must and mustn't for strong necessity, always followed by the base verb.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Wellbeing -- the state of feeling healthy, comfortable, and happy
"Good sleep is important for your wellbeing."
Balanced -- having the right amount of different things
"A balanced diet has fruit, vegetables, and protein."
Exhausted -- extremely tired, with no energy left
"After a double shift, I feel completely exhausted."
Stress -- a feeling of worry from a difficult or busy situation
"Too much stress can affect your health."
Symptom -- a sign in your body that shows you may be ill
"A bad headache can be a symptom of stress."
Checkup -- a medical exam to see if you are healthy
"You should book a checkup once a year."
To recover -- to get well again after being ill or very tired
"Your body needs time to recover after exercise."
Fit -- healthy and strong, usually from exercise
"Walking every day keeps you fit."
Mindful -- calmly aware of the present moment and how you feel
"Take a mindful minute and breathe slowly."
To avoid -- to keep away from something that could harm you
"You should avoid too much caffeine at night."
To cut down on -- to reduce the amount of something
"I need to cut down on sugar and coffee."
To cope with -- to deal successfully with a problem or with stress
"Exercise helps me cope with a busy week."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Wellbeing
Balanced
Exhausted
Stress
Symptom
Checkup
To recover
Fit
Mindful
To avoid
To cut down on
To cope with

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Ana Paula's friend Tom feels exhausted and full of stress. As a doctor, she gives him some advice. She says he should cut down on coffee and he should sleep more, because rest is important for his wellbeing. He should also eat a more balanced diet and take mindful breaks to cope with his busy days. But his headaches are a worrying symptom, so Ana Paula is firm: he must book a checkup, and he must not ignore the problem. If he follows this advice, he will soon feel fit and recover his energy.

1. Why do we say "he should sleep" and not "he should sleeps"?
A After should, we use the base verb, with no -s.
B "Sleep" has no third-person form.
C "Should" is only used with "I" and "you".
2. In "he must book a checkup", what does "must" show?
A A soft suggestion he can ignore.
B A strong necessity, almost a rule.
C Something that happened in the past.
3. Which sentence gives friendly advice correctly?
A "You should to rest more."
B "You should rest more."
C "You should resting more."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Should and Must

GRAMMAR

How to give advice (should) and show a strong necessity (must). After both, use the base verb.

ModalAffirmativeNegative & Question
should
(advice)		You should rest more.
a good idea		You shouldn't skip meals.
Should I see a doctor?
must
(strong necessity)		You must sleep more.
almost a rule		You mustn't ignore it.
= it is not allowed
After the modalAlways the base verb: no to, no -ing, no -s. "She should drink water" (not "drinks", not "to drink").

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"You look pale. You drink more water."
"This medicine is important. You take it every day."
"You are exhausted. You should this weekend."
"To sleep well, you should too much caffeine."
"Your symptoms are serious. You must a doctor today."
"A busy week is hard, but you should mindful breaks."

Stage 2: Put the Advice in Order

Order

Put the steps of giving good health advice in the correct order. Tap Listen to hear the full description.

?Say what they should avoid or cut down on.
?Give one final, kind piece of advice.
?Notice how your body feels and name the main symptom.
?Say what they must do if the problem is serious.
?Say what the person should do to feel better.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

You should get more sleep every night.
You mustn't ignore these symptoms.
You should cut down on coffee and sugar.
If the pain continues, you must see a doctor.
A balanced diet is good for your wellbeing.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real situation.

A friend has a bad cold. You give friendly advice:
A "You should to stay home."
B "You should stay home and rest."
C "You should staying home."
Your colleague has a very high fever. You say:
A "You must see a doctor today."
B "You must seeing a doctor today."
C "You must to see a doctor today."
You want to advise a friend against a bad habit. You say:
A "You shouldn't skip your meals."
B "You not should skip your meals."
C "You shouldn't to skip your meals."
You give advice about someone else (third person). Which is correct?
A "She should drinks more water."
B "She should drink more water."
C "She musts drink more water."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Someone in your life feels tired and stressed -- a patient, a friend, or a family member. Give them real health advice. Say what they should do to feel better, what they should avoid or cut down on, and what they must do if the problem is serious. Use should and must with the base verb. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 9

1You should get more sleep to protect your wellbeing.
2You should cut down on coffee and sugar.
3You must not ignore these symptoms.
4You should take short, mindful breaks during the day.
5If the pain continues, you must see a doctor.
Lesson 10 -- Pre-class

Plans & Intentions

Talk about the future. Key words: goal, intend, look forward to, upcoming, arrange, appointment, postpone, deadline, sign up, figure out, ahead, make up your mind. Grammar: going to for intentions, present continuous for fixed arrangements.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Twelve words to talk about your plans and the future. Listen to each one and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Goal -- something you want to achieve in the future
"My main goal is to speak English with confidence."
To intend -- to plan or mean to do something
"I intend to work as a doctor abroad."
To look forward to -- to feel excited about a future event
"I am looking forward to my trip in the spring."
Upcoming -- happening soon, in the near future
"I am a little nervous about my upcoming exam."
To arrange -- to plan and organize something in advance
"I arranged a meeting with my advisor."
Appointment -- a fixed time to meet someone or do something
"I have an appointment at the clinic on Monday."
To postpone -- to move an event to a later time
"We had to postpone the trip until June."
Deadline -- the latest time to finish something
"The deadline for the exam is in May."
To sign up -- to add your name to join a course or event
"I am going to sign up for a design course."
To figure out -- to find a solution or make a decision
"I need to figure out my next steps."
Ahead -- in the future, or coming soon in time
"There is a busy year ahead of me."
To make up my mind -- to make a final decision
"I finally made up my mind to move next year."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Goal
To intend
To look forward to
Upcoming
To arrange
Appointment
To postpone
Deadline
To sign up
To figure out
Ahead
To make up my mind

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

This year I made up my mind to be more organized. My main goal is to improve my English, so next month I am going to sign up for a course. I already arranged a study schedule: I am meeting my group every Tuesday evening. I also have an appointment with an advisor to figure out the next steps. The exam has a strict deadline, but I am really looking forward to the year ahead.

1. Which sentence is a plan or intention (going to)?
A "Next month I am going to sign up for a course."
B "Yesterday I am going to a course."
C "I go to sign up a course."
2. Which sentence is a fixed arrangement (present continuous)?
A "I am meeting my group every Tuesday evening."
B "I meet my group last week."
C "I will maybe meet my group."
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "I am going to study medicine."
B "I going to study medicine."
C "I am go to study medicine."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Two Ways to Talk About the Future

GRAMMAR

Use "going to" for intentions and plans, and the present continuous for fixed arrangements.

FormUse it forExample
Going to (+)plans and intentionsI am going to sign up for a course.
Going to (-)a plan you will not doI am not going to postpone the exam.
Going to (?)asking about plansWhat are you going to do next year?
Present continuousfixed arrangements with a time or placeI am meeting my advisor on Friday.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"Next month I am going to for a new course."
"My main this year is to pass the exam."
"I have an important with my advisor on Monday."
"The course has a strict for the final exam."
"On Friday I am my study group at six."
"I had to my trip until next month."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the sentences of the story in the correct order.

?Then I signed up for an advanced course with a strict deadline.
?This year I finally made up my mind to plan the future.
?Next month I have an important appointment to figure out the next steps.
?First, I wrote down my main goal for English.
?I arranged a study schedule and I am meeting my group every Tuesday.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

This year I am going to be more organized about my plans.
Next month I am signing up for an advanced course.
On Friday I am meeting my advisor at three.
I am really looking forward to the year ahead.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real situation.

A friend asks: "What are your plans for next year?" You answer:
A "Next year I am going to change jobs."
B "Next year I changed jobs yesterday."
C "Next year I am change jobs."
You want to talk about a fixed meeting on Friday. Which is correct?
A "On Friday I am meeting my advisor at three."
B "On Friday I meet my advisor last week."
C "On Friday I meeting my advisor."
You describe your main goal. Which is correct?
A "My goal is to pass the exam."
B "My goal is passed the exam."
C "My goal to passing the exam."
You need to move an event to a later time. You say:
A "I have to postpone the trip."
B "I have to deadline the trip."
C "I have to upcoming the trip."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Look at the year ahead. What are your main goals? What are you going to do to reach them? What fixed plans do you already have this month (with a day or a time)? Is there anything you had to postpone? Mix going to for your intentions and the present continuous for your arrangements. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 10

1This year I am going to be more organized about my plans.
2My main goal is to speak English with confidence.
3Next month I am signing up for a new course.
4I am really looking forward to the year ahead.
5I had to postpone my move, but it is the right choice.
Lesson 11 -- Pre-class

Predictions & Promises

Talking about the future in everyday life. Key words: prediction, to promise, to guess, to expect, to doubt, likely, certain, forecast, to wonder, to warn, spontaneous, to offer. Structure: will and won't for predictions, promises, and spontaneous decisions, always followed by the base verb.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Prediction -- a statement about what you think will happen in the future
"My prediction is that it will rain this afternoon."
To promise -- to say firmly that you will certainly do something
"I promise I will call you tonight."
To guess -- to give an answer when you are not completely sure
"I can't be sure, but I'll guess it's about five o'clock."
To expect -- to think that something will probably happen
"I expect the train will be a little late again."
To doubt -- to think that something is probably not true
"I doubt it will snow here in April."
Likely -- probably going to happen
"It is likely that prices will rise next year."
Certain -- completely sure about something
"I am certain she will win the game."
Forecast -- a report that says what will happen, especially the weather
"The forecast says it will be sunny tomorrow."
To wonder -- to think about something you are curious about
"I wonder if he will come to the party."
To warn -- to tell someone about a possible danger or problem
"I must warn you: the roads will be icy tonight."
Spontaneous -- decided suddenly, without planning it before
"It was a spontaneous decision to travel this weekend."
To offer -- to say you are happy to do something for someone
"She offered to drive me home after work."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Prediction
To promise
To guess
To expect
To doubt
Likely
Certain
Forecast
To wonder
To warn
Spontaneous
To offer

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Ana Paula is planning a weekend picnic, so she checks the weather forecast. The prediction is good: it will be sunny and warm on Saturday. She is certain the day will be perfect, and she expects at least ten friends. She doubts that anyone will stay home, because rain is not likely. Her friend Ben is nervous, so she makes a promise: she will bring a big salad and she will help him set everything up. She also has one small warning: their friend Chris will probably be late, as always. It was a spontaneous idea, but Ana Paula is sure it will be a wonderful afternoon.

1. Why do we say "it will rain" and not "it will rains"?
A After will, we use the base verb, with no -s.
B "Rain" has no third-person form.
C "Will" is only used with "it" and "you".
2. In "she will bring a big salad", what does "will" show here?
A A plan she made a long time ago.
B A promise or an offer to help.
C Something that happened in the past.
3. Which sentence makes a correct prediction?
A "I think it will to rain."
B "I think it will rain."
C "I think it will raining."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Will and Won't

GRAMMAR

Use will for predictions, promises, and decisions you make right now. After will, use the base verb.

UseAffirmativeNegative & Question
PredictionIt will rain later.
what you think happens		It won't rain today.
Will it rain?
Promise & decisionI will call you.
I'll help you now.		I won't forget.
Will you help me?
After willAlways the base verb: no to, no -ing, no -s. "She will call" (not "calls", not "to call"). In speech: I'll, she'll, they'll.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"Look at those clouds. I think it rain soon."
"Don't worry, I promise I be late."
"That box looks heavy. I carry it for you."
"The forecast is bad, so the game happen today."
"She studies hard, so I am certain she will the exam."
"In this traffic, I doubt they will before noon."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Put the steps of making a friendly prediction and a promise in the correct order. Tap Listen to hear the full description.

?Offer some help or make a promise with will.
?Start with what you notice or the weather forecast.
?End with a friendly warning or a kind word.
?Make your prediction using will.
?Say how certain you are, with "I am certain" or "I doubt".

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

I think it will be sunny this weekend.
I promise I will call you tonight.
I am certain the picnic will be great.
I doubt it will rain tomorrow.
That bag looks heavy. I will help you.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real situation.

You see dark clouds. You make a prediction:
A "I think it will raining."
B "I think it will rain soon."
C "I think it rains soon."
You promise a friend you will not be late. You say:
A "I promise I won't be late."
B "I promise I not will be late."
C "I promise I won't to be late."
Someone drops their books next to you. You offer help right now:
A "Wait, I'll help you."
B "Wait, I helping you."
C "Wait, I will helped you."
You make a prediction about someone else (third person). Which is correct?
A "She will calls you later."
B "She will call you later."
C "She wills call you later."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Look ahead one year. Make three real predictions about your life or your work using will. Say how certain you are, with certain, likely, expect, or doubt. Then finish with one promise you want to keep. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 11

1I think it will be sunny this weekend.
2I promise I will call you tonight.
3Do not worry, I will help you.
4I am certain it will be fine.
5I doubt it will rain, but I will bring an umbrella.
Lesson 12 -- Pre-class

Experiences So Far

Talk about your life experiences. Key words: abroad, opportunity, explore, highlight, accomplish, lifetime, volunteer, thrilling, attempt, brave, rewarding, bucket list. Grammar: present perfect with ever and never for life experiences.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Twelve words to talk about your life experiences. Listen to each one and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Abroad -- in or to a foreign country
"I have always wanted to work abroad."
Opportunity -- a chance to do something good
"Every trip is an opportunity to learn."
To explore -- to travel around a place to discover it
"We explored the old town on foot."
Highlight -- the best or most memorable part of an experience
"The safari was the highlight of the trip."
To accomplish -- to succeed in doing something difficult
"I have accomplished one of my biggest dreams."
Lifetime -- the whole period of a person's life
"It was the trip of a lifetime."
To volunteer -- to offer to help without being paid
"She has volunteered at a hospital."
Thrilling -- very exciting and a bit scary
"Riding a roller coaster is thrilling."
To attempt -- to try to do something difficult
"I have never attempted a marathon."
Brave -- ready to face danger or difficulty
"It was brave of you to try surfing."
Rewarding -- giving a feeling of satisfaction
"Helping others is very rewarding."
Bucket list -- a list of things you want to do in your life
"Seeing snow is on my bucket list."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Abroad
Opportunity
To explore
Highlight
To accomplish
Lifetime
To volunteer
Thrilling
To attempt
Brave
Rewarding
Bucket list

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

I have had an interesting life so far. I have traveled abroad several times, and I have explored many cities. Volunteering at a clinic was one of the most rewarding things I have ever done. I have attempted a few brave things, like diving, and each one was thrilling. But there are still things I have never done: I have never seen snow, so it is on my bucket list. One day I hope to accomplish that dream, the highlight of a lifetime.

1. Which sentence talks about a life experience (present perfect)?
A "I have traveled abroad several times."
B "I travel abroad yesterday."
C "I am travel abroad."
2. Which question asks about experience with "ever"?
A "Have you ever tried diving?"
B "Do you tried diving yesterday?"
C "Are you ever try diving?"
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "I have never seen snow."
B "I have never saw snow."
C "I never have see snow."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Perfect with Ever & Never

GRAMMAR

Use have or has + the past participle for life experiences. Add "ever" in questions and "never" for no experience.

FormUse it forExample
Present perfect (+)a life experienceI have traveled abroad.
(-) with neverno experienceI have never seen snow.
(?) with everasking about experienceHave you ever tried diving?
Short answersa quick replyYes, I have. / No, I have not.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"I have many beautiful cities."
"Volunteering is the most thing I have ever done."
"I have never abroad."
"Seeing snow is on my ."
"Have you ever something thrilling?"
"One day I want to my biggest dream."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the sentences of the story in the correct order.

?There, I explored an old city, and it became the highlight of my trip.
?I have always loved trying new things in my life.
?Now, seeing the northern lights is the only thing left on my bucket list.
?A few years ago, I finally traveled abroad for the first time.
?Later, I attempted something brave and went diving in the sea.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

I have traveled abroad several times.
The highlight of my life was climbing a mountain.
I have never seen snow, but it is on my bucket list.
Have you ever done anything thrilling?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real situation.

A friend asks: "Have you ever been abroad?" You answer:
A "Yes, I have been to Portugal and Italy."
B "Yes, I go there tomorrow."
C "Yes, I am been there."
You want to talk about an experience you have never had. Which is correct?
A "I have never gone skydiving."
B "I have never went skydiving."
C "I never gone skydiving."
You describe the best part of a trip. You say:
A "The safari was the highlight of the trip."
B "The safari was the deadline of the trip."
C "The safari was the bucket of the trip."
You ask a friend about their life experience. You say:
A "Have you ever volunteered?"
B "Did you ever volunteering?"
C "Have you ever volunteer?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Look back at your life so far. What are some of the most interesting things you have done? Where have you traveled, and what have you explored? Is there anything rewarding you have done to help others? Now think of two or three things you have never done but would love to try one day. Use the present perfect (I have..., I have never...) and take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 12

1I have traveled abroad several times.
2The most rewarding thing I have ever done was to volunteer.
3I have never seen snow, but it is on my bucket list.
4Have you ever done anything really thrilling?
5It was the trip of a lifetime.
Lesson 13 -- Pre-class

Midpoint Review -- How Far Have You Come?

A midpoint review of your first twelve lessons. Key words: journey, routine, neighborhood, hobby, schedule, goal, milestone, opportunity, abroad, highlight, overcome, bucket list. Grammar: review of tenses -- present, past, present perfect and future.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Twelve key words from your first twelve lessons. Listen to each one and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Journey -- a long trip, or the process of learning and growing over time
"Learning English has been an amazing journey."
Routine -- the things you usually do every day
"My morning routine starts at six."
Neighborhood -- the area around your home where you live
"I live in a quiet neighborhood."
Hobby -- an activity you do for fun in your free time
"Reading is my favorite hobby."
Schedule -- a plan of when things happen during your day or week
"My schedule is very busy this week."
Goal -- something you want to achieve in the future
"My goal is to speak English fluently."
Milestone -- an important moment or achievement in your life
"Finishing school was a big milestone."
Opportunity -- a chance to do something good
"Every class is an opportunity to learn."
Abroad -- in or to a foreign country
"I want to work abroad one day."
Highlight -- the best or most memorable part of an experience
"The trip was the highlight of my year."
To overcome -- to succeed in dealing with a problem or difficulty
"I have overcome my fear of speaking."
Bucket list -- a list of things you want to do in your life
"Seeing snow is on my bucket list."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Journey
Routine
Neighborhood
Hobby
Schedule
Goal
Milestone
Opportunity
Abroad
Highlight
To overcome
Bucket list

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions. Notice how one story uses many tenses.

When I started this course, I could only say a few words, and speaking felt scary. These days I have a busy routine, but I study almost every day. Learning English has been a long journey, and every class is an opportunity to grow. So far, I have reached a few milestones and I have overcome many mistakes. In the future, I am going to work abroad, and I will keep practicing until my goal comes true.

1. Which sentence talks about the START of her journey (past simple)?
A "When I started, I could only say a few words."
B "These days I study every day."
C "One day I am going to work abroad."
2. Which sentence talks about her journey SO FAR (present perfect)?
A "I have overcome many mistakes."
B "I started the course last year."
C "I study English every day."
3. Which sentence talks about a future plan?
A "I am going to work abroad."
B "I have reached a few milestones."
C "When I started, I felt nervous."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- One Story, Many Tenses

GRAMMAR

When you review your progress, you mix tenses. Choose each one by the TIME you mean, not by the word.

TenseUse it forExample
Past simplethe beginning, a finished timeI started, I learned.
Present simpleyour life now, routinesI study every day.
Present perfectthe journey so farI have overcome many mistakes.
Future (going to / will)plans and predictionsI am going to work abroad.
Modals (can)abilityI can hold a conversation.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"Learning English has been a long ."
"I study every day as part of my ."
"Every class is an to grow."
"Speaking for one minute was a big for me."
"My is to work abroad as a doctor."
"Seeing snow is on my ."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the sentences of the story in the correct order.

?Later, I talked about my goals and the milestones I want to reach.
?When I started this course, I could only say a few words.
?Now I am ready for the second half of my English journey.
?In the first lessons, I learned to describe my neighborhood and my daily routine.
?Along the way, I have overcome many of my mistakes.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

Learning English has been a long journey.
So far, I have overcome many mistakes.
One day, I am going to work abroad.
Now I can hold a real conversation.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real situation.

You talk about the very start of your course. You say:
A "When I started, I knew only a few words."
B "When I start, I know only a few words."
C "When I am starting, I am knowing a few words."
You describe your English now. You say:
A "These days, I study almost every day."
B "These days, I studied almost every day."
C "These days, I will study almost every day."
You talk about your journey so far. You say:
A "I have reached a few important milestones."
B "I reach a few important milestones."
C "I am reach a few important milestones."
You talk about a plan for the second half. You say:
A "Next, I am going to work on my pronunciation."
B "Next, I go to work on my pronunciation yesterday."
C "Next, I have worked on my pronunciation tomorrow."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Tell the story of your English so far. Where did you start, and how did you feel? What is your routine now, and what have you overcome? What are your goals and your bucket list for the second half of the course? Mix the past, the present, the present perfect and the future, and take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 13

1When I started, I could only say a few words.
2Learning English has been a long journey.
3I have overcome many of my mistakes.
4Now I can hold a real conversation.
5One day, I am going to work abroad.
Lesson 14 -- Pre-class

Phrasal Verbs in Real Life

Everyday phrasal verbs for your daily routine and social life. Key verbs: wake up, get up, run into, look after, give up, turn down, fill out, drop off, pick up, sort out, put off, get along. Grammar: phrasal verbs, separable and inseparable.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Twelve everyday phrasal verbs for your daily routine and social life. Listen to each one and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

To wake up -- to stop sleeping
"I wake up at six o'clock every day."
To get up -- to leave your bed after you wake up
"I do not get up right away."
To run into -- to meet someone by chance
"I ran into an old friend at the store."
To look after -- to take care of someone or something
"I look after my daughter in the evening."
To give up -- to stop trying to do something
"I never give up when things get hard."
To turn down -- to say no to an offer or invitation
"I had to turn down the invitation."
To fill out -- to complete a form with your information
"I filled out three long forms."
To drop off -- to take someone somewhere and leave them there
"I drop off my daughter at school."
To pick up -- to go and collect someone or something
"I pick up my daughter after work."
To sort out -- to solve a problem or put things in order
"I need to sort out my schedule."
To put off -- to move something to a later time
"Do not put off your rest."
To get along -- to have a friendly relationship with someone
"I get along well with my neighbors."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each phrasal verb with the correct definition.

To wake up
To get up
To run into
To look after
To give up
To turn down
To fill out
To drop off
To pick up
To sort out
To put off
To get along

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Every weekday I wake up at six, but I do not get up right away. When I finally get up, I drop off my daughter at school.
