At the hospital I fill out forms and sort out small problems, and I never give up. On my way home I sometimes run into a neighbor, and we get along really well. In the evening I pick up my daughter and look after her. If I am tired, I turn down invitations, because I do not like to put off my rest.

1. Which sentence uses a phrasal verb correctly?
A "I drop off my daughter at school."
B "I drop my daughter school."
C "I dropping off my daughter."
2. "I run into a neighbor" means...
A I meet the neighbor by chance.
B I hit the neighbor with my car.
C I run away from the neighbor.
3. Which sentence puts the pronoun in the right place?
A "I pick her up after work."
B "I pick up her after work."
C "I pick after work her up."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Separable & Inseparable Phrasal Verbs

GRAMMAR

A phrasal verb is a verb plus a small word (up, out, off, into) with a new meaning. Some are separable (an object can go in the middle), and some are inseparable (the words stay together). A pronoun always goes in the middle of a separable verb.

TypeRuleExample
Separableobject can go in the middleI filled out the form. / I filled the form out.
Separable + pronounpronoun MUST go in the middleI picked her up. (not "picked up her")
Inseparablethe words stay togetherI ran into a friend. (not "ran a friend into")
Meaninglearn it as one wordlook after = take care of

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct verb. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"I up early every day."
"I off my daughter at school."
"I had to out three forms."
"I into an old friend yesterday."
"I after my daughter in the evening."
"I never up when things get hard."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the sentences of the story in the correct order.

?At work, I fill out forms and sort out small problems.
?On a busy morning, I wake up before my alarm.
?In the evening, I pick up my daughter and look after her.
?I get up, make coffee, and drop off my daughter at school.
?On my way home, I run into a friend at the store.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

I wake up early, but I do not get up right away.
I ran into an old friend at the pharmacy.
I pick up my daughter and look after her.
I get along really well with my neighbors.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real situation.

A friend asks what time your day starts. You say:
A "I get up at six every morning."
B "I get on at six every morning."
C "I get six up morning."
You met an old colleague by chance today. You tell a friend:
A "I ran into an old colleague today."
B "I ran an old colleague into today."
C "I ran into today an old colleague."
You are too tired to accept an invitation. You say:
A "Thanks, but I have to turn down your invitation tonight."
B "Thanks, but I have to turn on your invitation tonight."
C "Thanks, but I have to fill out your invitation tonight."
You want to say you have a good relationship with your neighbors. You say:
A "I get along really well with my neighbors."
B "I give up really well with my neighbors."
C "I put off really well with my neighbors."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Walk me through a normal day, from morning to night. What time do you wake up and get up? Who do you drop off or pick up? At work, what do you sort out or fill out? Do you ever run into people you know? In the evening, who do you look after, and do you sometimes turn down invitations? Use as many phrasal verbs as you can, and take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 14

1I wake up early, but I do not get up right away.
2I drop off my daughter and pick her up after work.
3I had to fill out a lot of forms this week.
4I ran into an old friend and we get along well.
5I do not like to put off my rest.
Lesson 15 -- Pre-class

Making Your Voice Heard

Sharing your opinion in everyday conversation. Key words: point of view, to bring up, to point out, to admit, honestly, actually, to suppose, to insist, to convince, to disagree, to argue, biased. Function: how to give your opinion, agree, disagree politely, and soften your words (hedging).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Point of view -- the way you think about a subject; your opinion
"From my point of view, both cities are great places to live."
To bring up -- to start talking about a topic
"She brought up an interesting idea during dinner."
To point out -- to mention a fact that others may not have noticed
"He pointed out that the plan had one small problem."
To admit -- to agree that something is true, even if you do not want to
"I have to admit that you were right about the movie."
Honestly -- used to show you are saying what you really think
"Honestly, I did not enjoy the book very much."
Actually -- used to add a fact or gently correct someone
"Actually, I think the second option is better."
To suppose -- to think that something is probably true
"I suppose you are right, but I still have doubts."
To insist -- to say very firmly that something is true or must happen
"My brother insists that his team is the best."
To convince -- to make someone believe or agree with you
"She convinced me to try the new restaurant."
To disagree -- to have a different opinion from someone
"I respect your idea, but I disagree with it."
To argue -- to give reasons for or against an idea
"Some people argue that working from home is better."
Biased -- showing an unfair preference for one side; not neutral
"The review felt biased, so I did not fully trust it."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Point of view
To bring up
To point out
To admit
Honestly
Actually
To suppose
To insist
To convince
To disagree
To argue
Biased

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

At dinner, Ana Paula's friend brought up a question: is it better to live in a big city or a small town? "In my opinion, a big city is more exciting," she said. Honestly, her friend did not agree. "I see your point of view, but I disagree," he replied. "Actually, I suppose a small town is calmer and safer." Ana Paula pointed out that cities have more jobs, and she had to admit that was important for her career. Her friend insisted that peace matters more, and he tried to convince her. Neither of them was biased; they simply saw it differently. "You could argue both sides," Ana Paula laughed. "Let's just agree to disagree."

1. Which phrase does Ana Paula use to give her opinion?
A "In my opinion, a big city is more exciting."
B "I have to go to the city."
C "The city is at eight o'clock."
2. How does her friend disagree politely?
A "You are completely wrong."
B "I see your point of view, but I disagree."
C "Stop talking about the city."
3. What does "she had to admit" show about Ana Paula?
A She refused to accept the other idea.
B She accepted a true point, even if she did not want to.
C She was not listening at all.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Language of Opinions

GRAMMAR

There is no new tense here. Instead, you learn set phrases to give an opinion, agree, disagree politely, and soften your words. Keep them ready and natural.

FunctionUseful phrases
Give an opinionIn my opinion, ... · I think (that) ... · If you ask me, ... · From my point of view, ...
AgreeI totally agree. · That is a good point. · Exactly. · You are right.
Disagree politelyI see your point, but ... · I am not sure I agree. · I am afraid I disagree. · Actually, I think ...
Soften (hedging)I suppose ... · It seems to me ... · kind of / sort of · to be honest ...

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"In my , both plans are good."
"I see your point, I still disagree."
"I have to that you were right."
"That is a good , I had not thought of that."
"Honestly, I am not I agree with that."
"Actually, I you are right about that."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Put the steps of sharing an opinion politely in the correct order. Tap Listen to hear the full description.

?Add a reason or point out a fact that supports your idea.
?Bring up the topic and say what you are talking about.
?Soften your words or find common ground to keep it friendly.
?Give your opinion clearly, with "In my opinion" or "I think".
?Listen to the other person and agree or disagree politely.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

In my opinion, a big city is more exciting.
I see your point, but I disagree.
Honestly, I am not sure I agree.
That is a good point, I had not thought of that.
Actually, I suppose you are right about that.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real situation.

A friend asks what you think about a new restaurant. You give your opinion:
A "The restaurant is a place."
B "In my opinion, the food is great but a bit expensive."
C "I will restaurant tomorrow."
You do not agree, but you want to be polite. You say:
A "I see your point, but I am not sure I agree."
B "That is a stupid idea."
C "You are always wrong."
You strongly agree with your colleague's idea. You say:
A "Maybe, I do not know."
B "Exactly, that is a really good point."
C "Let us talk about something else."
You want to soften your opinion so it sounds friendly. You say:
A "This is the only correct answer."
B "It seems to me the first plan is a little better."
C "You must do what I say."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think of a topic you have an opinion about (a movie, your city, working from home, or social media). Give your point of view clearly, add one reason, and then say what someone who disagrees might argue. Finish by softening your words or finding common ground. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 15

1In my opinion, this is the best option.
2That is a good point, I agree.
3I see your point, but I disagree.
4Honestly, I am not sure about that.
5Actually, I suppose you are right.
Lesson 16 -- Pre-class

Saying More With Less

Everyday conversation vocabulary and the short words that keep a chat flowing. Key words: small talk, chat, casual, awkward, interrupt, nod, mention, brief, leave out, sum up, likewise, go on. Grammar: ellipsis and substitution -- so, one, do so, neither and either.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Twelve everyday words for natural conversation. Listen to each one and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Small talk -- light, friendly conversation about the small, everyday things in life
"We made small talk about the weather."
To chat -- to talk with someone in a friendly, relaxed way
"We chatted for a few minutes at the door."
Casual -- relaxed, friendly and not formal
"It was just a casual conversation, nothing serious."
Awkward -- uncomfortable or slightly embarrassing
"There was an awkward silence for a moment."
To interrupt -- to stop someone while they are speaking
"Sorry to interrupt, but I have a quick question."
To nod -- to move your head up and down to show you agree
"She nodded to show she understood."
To mention -- to say something briefly, without many details
"He mentioned that he was a little tired."
Brief -- short, using only a little time or a few words
"We had a brief chat before the meeting."
To leave out -- to not include something
"In fast talk, we leave out the obvious words."
To sum up -- to say the main points in a short, clear way
"To sum up, we agreed on almost everything."
Likewise -- in the same way; the same is true for me
"She was glad to help, and likewise, so was I."
To go on -- to continue talking or doing something
"Please go on, I am listening."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each conversation word with the correct definition.

Small talk
To chat
Casual
Awkward
To interrupt
To nod
To mention
Brief
To leave out
To sum up
Likewise
To go on

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

I had a brief chat with my neighbor at the bakery. When she said she loved the coffee, I did not repeat the whole sentence; I just said, "So do I." She asked if the shop was open on Sunday, and I answered, "I think so," instead of the long version. She wanted a small cake, so the server asked, "Which one?" When she mentioned that she does not like big parties, I nodded and said, "Neither do I." We left the obvious words out, and the conversation felt light and natural.

1. "I think so" is a short way to say...
A "I think the shop is open on Sunday."
B "I do not know the answer."
C "I am thinking about something."
2. Your friend says, "I love this song." You feel the same. You say:
A "So do I."
B "So am I."
C "So I do."
3. Your friend says, "I do not like the cold." You agree. You say:
A "Neither do I."
B "So do I."
C "Me too do I."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Ellipsis & Substitution

GRAMMAR

To sound natural, English often leaves out words you can guess and replaces them with one short word. Learn the four short words below. After "so" and "neither" at the start, the verb comes before the subject (So do I, Neither can I).

Short wordReplacesExample
soa whole idea"Is it open?" -- "I think so."
one / onesa noun"Which cake?" -- "The small one."
do / does / dida verb phrase"I love it." -- "So do I."
neither / eithera negative idea"I cannot go." -- "Neither can I."

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each short answer with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"I love this coffee. So I."
"Is the pharmacy open? I think ."
"She wanted a small cake, so she chose the small ."
"I do not like big parties, and does my sister."
"He was tired, and so I."
"You helped me a lot. , thank you for your time."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the sentences of the story in the correct order.

?When she said she loved the coffee, I said, So do I.
?I met my neighbor Rita for a brief chat at the bakery.
?To sum up, the whole conversation felt easy and natural.
?At first we just made small talk about the weekend.
?She asked if the shop was open on Sunday, and I said, I think so.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each short answer, then record yourself saying it.

So do I. I love this place too.
Neither do I. I do not like big crowds.
I think so, but I am not completely sure.
Which cake would you like? The small one, please.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the most natural short answer for each real situation.

A friend says, "I really enjoy quiet mornings." You feel the same. You say:
A "So do I."
B "So I enjoy."
C "I do so enjoy it."
A colleague says, "I have not tried that new cafe yet." You have not either. You say:
A "Neither have I."
B "So have I."
C "Me too have I."
At a shop, the server asks, "Which bag would you like?" You want the blue bag. You say:
A "The blue one, please."
B "The blue, please."
C "The one blue bag please one."
Someone asks, "Do you think the train is on time?" You are fairly sure it is. You say:
A "I think so."
B "I think it."
C "I so think."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think about a friendly chat you had recently -- with a neighbor, a colleague or someone at a shop. Describe it in English. What small talk did you make? When did you agree? Try to use short answers instead of repeating whole sentences: "So do I," "Neither do I," "I think so," "The small one." Take your time and speak naturally.

Survival Card -- Lesson 16

1So do I. I feel exactly the same.
2Neither do I. I do not enjoy that either.
3I think so, but I am not sure.
4I would like the small one, please.
5To sum up, we agreed on almost everything.
Lesson 17 -- Pre-class

Numbers That Speak

Talking about data and research findings in everyday conversation. Key words: data, average, percentage, to increase, to decrease, rate, survey, result, figure, trend, to estimate, roughly. Function: how to read numbers aloud, describe trends, and share results clearly.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Data -- facts and numbers collected for information or study
"The doctor studied the data from hundreds of patients."
Average -- the typical amount, found by adding numbers and dividing
"The average age of the group was thirty-five."
Percentage -- an amount expressed as a part of one hundred
"A large percentage of people said they felt better."
To increase -- to become larger in number or amount
"The number of visitors increased last year."
To decrease -- to become smaller in number or amount
"Prices decreased a little after the holidays."
Rate -- how often or how fast something happens, as a number
"The success rate of the treatment was very high."
Survey -- a set of questions asked to many people to collect answers
"The survey showed that most people exercise weekly."
Result -- the information you get at the end of a study or test
"The results of the study surprised everyone."
Figure -- a number, especially in official information or statistics
"The latest figures show a small rise in cases."
Trend -- a general direction in which something is changing over time
"There is a growing trend toward remote work."
To estimate -- to guess a number or amount using the information you have
"Experts estimate that thousands of people will attend."
Roughly -- approximately; not exactly
"The trip takes roughly two hours."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Data
Average
Percentage
To increase
To decrease
Rate
Survey
Result
Figure
Trend
To estimate
Roughly

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Last week, Ana Paula read a health survey with some surprising results. The data showed that the average adult sleeps only six and a half hours a night. According to the figures, the percentage of tired people has increased over the last ten years. One in four adults said they rarely feel rested. On the other hand, the number of people who exercise has decreased a little, but a new trend is starting: short daily walks. The rate of people walking to work went up by roughly five percent. From her own patients, Ana Paula could estimate that these numbers were true. "The results are clear," she thought. "Small changes really matter."

1. What does the data show about how much the average adult sleeps?
A Only about six and a half hours a night.
B Exactly ten hours a night.
C The survey did not mention sleep.
2. What happened to the percentage of tired people?
A It decreased a lot.
B It increased over the last ten years.
C It stayed exactly the same.
3. What new trend is starting, according to the text?
A People are sleeping much more.
B More people are taking short daily walks.
C Fewer people answer surveys.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Language of Numbers

GRAMMAR

There is no new tense here. Instead, you learn how to read numbers aloud, describe how they change, and stay approximate. Keep these phrases ready and natural.

FunctionUseful phrases
Read numbers aloud45% = "forty-five percent" · 1 in 3 = "one in three" · 2.5 = "two point five"
Say how manya large number of ... · the majority of ... · one in four ... · on average, ...
Describe changego up / increase / rise · go down / decrease / fall · sharply / slightly
Stay approximateroughly ... · about / around ... · nearly ... · over / more than ...

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"The data shows a clear toward reading on screens."
"A large of people agreed."
"On , adults sleep about seven hours."
"The number of readers last year."
"We that thousands of people will attend."
"The whole trip takes two hours."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Put the steps of sharing data clearly in the correct order. Tap Listen to hear the full description.

?Describe the trend and say if the number went up or down.
?Say what the data is about and where the numbers come from.
?Estimate what might happen next or explain why the numbers matter.
?Give the main figure, like a percentage or an average.
?Add one more result that supports your point.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

The data shows a clear trend.
About sixty percent of people agreed.
On average, adults sleep seven hours.
The number of readers increased slightly.
Roughly one in three people said yes.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real situation.

You want to present the main number from a study. You say:
A "The number is a number thing."
B "On average, patients waited about thirty minutes."
C "I average the patients tomorrow."
A figure went from 40% to 55%. How do you describe it?
A "The percentage increased from forty to fifty-five percent."
B "The percentage decreased a lot."
C "The percentage stayed the same."
You are not sure of the exact number, so you stay approximate. You say:
A "It is exactly 1,247 people, no more, no less."
B "Roughly one thousand people took the survey."
C "Some people, I do not know how."
You describe a general direction of change over time. You say:
A "There is a survey of the numbers."
B "There is a growing trend toward working from home."
C "The trend is a number I read."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think of some numbers you know well (from your work, your city, or an article you read). Share one clear finding: say what the data is about, give the main figure, describe the trend, and estimate why it matters. Use "on average", "roughly", and "the percentage increased/decreased". Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 17

1The data shows a clear trend.
2On average, it is about seven hours.
3The percentage increased since last year.
4Roughly one in three people agreed.
5I estimate the number will keep rising.
Lesson 18 -- Pre-class

Navigating the Unexpected

Everyday words for clarifying and the repair strategies that keep you in control when a conversation is confusing. Key words: clarify, rephrase, catch, confusing, make sense, vague, hesitate, filler, recap, bear with, hold on, straightforward. Grammar: clarification and repair strategies -- asking for repetition, buying time and checking you understood.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Twelve everyday words for staying clear when a conversation is hard. Listen to each one and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

To clarify -- to make something clearer or easier to understand
"Could you clarify what you mean?"
To rephrase -- to say the same thing again using different words
"Let me rephrase that more simply."
To catch -- to hear and understand what someone says
"Sorry, I did not catch your name."
Confusing -- hard to understand; not clear
"The directions were a little confusing."
To make sense -- to be clear and easy to understand
"Now your explanation makes sense."
Vague -- not clear or not exact
"His answer was vague, so I asked again."
To hesitate -- to pause before speaking because you are not sure
"Do not hesitate to ask me to repeat."
Filler -- a small word like well or um that gives you time to think
"Well is a common filler in English."
To recap -- to repeat the main points quickly
"Let me recap what we decided."
To bear with -- to ask someone to be patient and wait
"Bear with me while I find the word."
To hold on -- to wait for a short moment
"Hold on a second, please."
Straightforward -- simple, clear and easy to understand
"Her instructions were straightforward."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each clarifying word with the correct definition.

To clarify
To rephrase
To catch
Confusing
To make sense
Vague
To hesitate
Filler
To recap
To bear with
To hold on
Straightforward

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

On the phone, the clinic spoke fast and I could not catch the time. Instead of pretending, I asked, "Could you say that again?" When a word was confusing, I said, "What do you mean by that?" I needed a moment, so I used a filler -- "Well, let me think for a second" -- to hold on and think. At the end, just to recap, I repeated the address back to check I understood. None of this is rude; it keeps the conversation straightforward.

1. "Could you say that again?" is a polite way to...
A ask someone to repeat something.
B end the conversation.
C change the subject.
2. You do not know a word. The best question is:
A "What do you mean by that?"
B "Why you talk?"
C "So do I."
3. A "filler" like "well" or "let me see" helps you...
A buy a little time to think.
B finish the conversation faster.
C correct your grammar.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Clarification & Repair Strategies

GRAMMAR

When you do not understand or need a moment, you have four simple tools. Keep the tone polite: start with "Sorry" or "Could you", and the request sounds friendly, never rude.

FunctionPhraseExample
Ask to repeatCould you say that again?"Sorry, I did not catch that. Could you say that again?"
Ask for meaningWhat do you mean by...?"What do you mean by 'checkup'?"
Buy timeLet me think for a second."That is a good question -- let me think for a second."
Check / recapSo, just to recap...?"So, just to recap, we meet at three, right?"

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"Sorry, I did not your name. Could you say it again?"
"That word is new to me. Could you it in simpler words?"
"Can you on for a second, please?"
"So, just to , the meeting is at three."
"Her directions were , so I asked her to explain again."
"Now your explanation makes to me."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the sentences of the story in the correct order.

?I asked her to clarify and to rephrase it in simpler words.
?Last week I called a clinic to book a checkup.
?To recap, I repeated the address back to check I understood.
?The receptionist spoke fast, and I did not catch the time.
?I used a filler to hold on and think for a second.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Sorry, I did not catch that. Could you say it again?
What do you mean by that, exactly?
Let me think for a second before I answer.
So, just to recap, we meet at three, right?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the most natural thing to say in each real situation.

Someone gives you a phone number too fast. What do you say?
A "Sorry, could you say that again?"
B "Yes, thank you, goodbye."
C "I number not have."
A word in the conversation is new to you and you want its meaning. You say:
A "What do you mean by that?"
B "So do I."
C "I mean nothing."
You need a moment to think about a hard question. You say:
A "Let me think for a second."
B "Repeat now, please."
C "I think so fast."
The instructions were long and you want to check you understood. You say:
A "So, just to recap, is that right?"
B "I recap you now."
C "Neither do I."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think about a time when you did not understand someone -- on the phone, at a store or in a fast conversation. Describe it in English. What was confusing? What could you say next time to stay in control? Try to use the repair strategies from this lesson: "Sorry, could you say that again?", "What do you mean by that?", "Let me think for a second", "So, just to recap...". Take your time and speak naturally.

Survival Card -- Lesson 18

1Sorry, could you say that again?
2What do you mean by that, exactly?
3Let me think for a second.
4Could you rephrase that in simpler words?
5So, just to recap, is that correct?
Lesson 19 -- Pre-class

The Language of Professional Identity

Introducing yourself as a medical professional in transition. Key words: field, qualification, to specialize, expertise, to pursue, transition, colleague, license, degree, residency, to adapt, demanding. Function: how to describe your career background and current situation clearly and naturally.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Field -- the area of work or study a person is trained in
"Her field is medicine, and mine is engineering."
Qualification -- an official record that shows you finished training or passed an exam
"You need the right qualifications to work as a nurse."
To specialize -- to focus on one particular area of your work
"She decided to specialize in children's health."
Expertise -- special skill or deep knowledge in a particular area
"His expertise in surgery is respected by everyone."
To pursue -- to work toward a goal steadily over a long time
"He left his old job to pursue a career in research."
Transition -- the process of changing from one situation to another
"The transition to a new country was difficult but exciting."
Colleague -- a person you work with
"My colleagues at the clinic are very supportive."
License -- official permission to work in a profession
"A doctor needs a license to treat patients here."
Degree -- a qualification you receive from a university
"She has a degree in medicine from a Brazilian university."
Residency -- a period of supervised hospital training for a doctor after medical school
"During his residency, he worked long night shifts."
To adapt -- to change your behavior to fit a new situation
"It takes time to adapt to a new health system."
Demanding -- needing a lot of time, effort, or skill
"Medicine is a demanding but rewarding profession."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Field
Qualification
To specialize
Expertise
To pursue
Transition
Colleague
License
Degree
Residency
To adapt
Demanding

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Ana Paula is a doctor going through a big transition. She earned her medical degree in Brazil and has worked as a physician for more than ten years. Her main field is family medicine, and she recently began to specialize in care for older patients. Now she lives in the United States, where she is pursuing a new license to practice. The process is demanding: she has passed two exams already, but she still studies every night. Her new colleagues value her expertise, and slowly she is learning to adapt to a different health system. "My qualifications travel with me," she says. "My experience never disappears."

1. Where did Ana Paula earn her medical degree?
A In Brazil.
B In the United States.
C The text does not say.
2. What is she doing now in the United States?
A She has stopped working as a doctor forever.
B She is pursuing a new license to practice.
C She is studying to become an engineer.
3. Why does she say "my qualifications travel with me"?
A Because she travels a lot for work.
B Because her training and experience stay with her in any country.
C Because she lost her old documents.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Background & Now

GRAMMAR

To describe your professional identity, you mix three tenses. Use the present perfect to link your past to now (how long, what you have achieved), the past simple for finished moments, and the present simple for who you are today.

UseExample
Present perfect (past → now)"I have worked as a doctor for ten years." · "I have just passed my exam."
Past simple (finished time)"I studied medicine in Brazil." · "I graduated in 2012."
Present simple (who I am now)"I am a family doctor." · "I work at a clinic in Boston."
Present continuous (right now)"I am pursuing a new license." · "I am learning the system."

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"I as a doctor for more than ten years."
"My main is family medicine."
"I am going through a career ."
"I medicine in Brazil."
"I recently started to in care for older patients."
"It takes time to to a new health system."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Put the steps of a clear professional introduction in the correct order. Tap Listen to hear the full description.

?Say how long you have worked and what you specialize in.
?Say your name and your field, so people know who you are.
?Say what you are working toward now and what comes next.
?Give your main qualification and where you studied.
?Explain your current situation or the transition you are going through.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

I have worked as a doctor for ten years.
My field is family medicine.
I am going through a career transition.
I am pursuing a new license here.
My colleagues value my expertise.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real situation.

A new colleague asks what you do. You say:
A "I am doctor thing in the medicine."
B "I am a doctor. My field is family medicine."
C "I doctor since many years ago."
You want to say how long you have been a doctor (from the past until now). You say:
A "I have worked as a doctor for twelve years."
B "I work as a doctor since twelve years."
C "I am working doctor twelve years ago."
You explain your current situation in a new country. You say:
A "I pursued a license, and it finished yesterday forever."
B "Right now, I am pursuing a new license to practice."
C "I license the practice new."
You describe your training background. You say:
A "I have studied medicine in Brazil in 2012."
B "I studied medicine in Brazil and did my residency there."
C "I study medicine yesterday in Brazil."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Introduce yourself as a professional. Say your name and your field, give your main qualification and where you studied, say how long you have worked and what you specialize in, and explain your current situation or transition. Use the present perfect for "how long", the past simple for finished moments, and the present simple for who you are now. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 19

1I am a doctor, and my field is family medicine.
2I have worked as a physician for over ten years.
3I studied medicine in Brazil and did my residency there.
4Right now, I am pursuing my license here.
5A career transition takes time, but experience stays.
Lesson 20 -- Pre-class

Fluency Under Pressure

The milestone lesson: keep a real conversation going without freezing. Key words: fluency, confident, effortless, stumble, elaborate, pace, express, keep up, tongue-tied, get across, breakthrough, sustain. Focus: sustained fluency and spontaneous production -- paraphrase a missing word, buy a second, and keep the conversation flowing.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Twelve words about speaking freely and keeping a conversation alive. Listen to each one and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Fluency -- the ability to speak smoothly and easily
"Your fluency has improved so much this year."
Confident -- sure of yourself and not nervous
"I feel confident speaking English now."
Effortless -- so easy that it needs no effort
"After practice, small talk feels effortless."
To stumble -- to stop or make a mistake while speaking
"I still stumble on long words sometimes."
To elaborate -- to explain something in more detail
"Could you elaborate on that idea?"
Pace -- how fast or slow you speak
"Try a calm, steady pace when you talk."
To express -- to say what you think or feel
"I can express my opinion more clearly now."
To keep up -- to follow a fast conversation without falling behind
"I can keep up with native speakers now."
Tongue-tied -- unable to speak because you are nervous
"I used to get tongue-tied in meetings."
To get across -- to make people understand your idea
"I got my point across without any trouble."
Breakthrough -- a sudden, important improvement
"Speaking for a whole minute was a real breakthrough."
To sustain -- to keep something going for a long time
"I can sustain a conversation for several minutes."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each fluency word with the correct definition.

Fluency
Confident
Effortless
To stumble
To elaborate
Pace
To express
To keep up
Tongue-tied
To get across
Breakthrough
To sustain

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

A year ago I always stumbled on hard words and felt tongue-tied. I translated every sentence in my head, so I could never keep up with a fast conversation. Then I stopped waiting for the perfect sentence. When I forgot a word, I expressed the idea in other words and kept going. I learned to speak at a calm pace and to sustain a chat for several minutes. Last week I got my point across for ten whole minutes -- that was a real breakthrough, and it felt almost effortless.

1. To "keep up" with a conversation means to...
A follow it without falling behind.
B end it quickly.
C translate every word.
2. When Ana Paula forgets a word, the best thing to do is...
A describe the idea in other words and keep going.
B stop and stay silent.
C start the sentence again in Portuguese.
3. For Ana Paula, a "breakthrough" was...
A speaking freely for ten minutes without translating.
B learning one new grammar rule.
C making zero mistakes.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Fluency Toolkit

GRAMMAR

Spontaneous production means speaking without a script. When something is missing, you do not stop -- you use one of four simple moves and keep the conversation flowing.

MovePhraseExample
Paraphrase a missing wordWhat I mean is... / It is the thing you use to..."I forgot the word -- it is the thing you use to cook."
Buy a secondGive me a second. / Let me think."That is a good question -- give me a second."
Pass the turn backWhat about you? / Tell me more."It was great. What about you?"
Add detail (elaborate)For example... / because..."I love it, for example, when it saves me time."

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"I can now a conversation for several minutes."
"When I forget a word, I the idea in other words."
"I used to feel in every meeting."
"Speaking for ten minutes was a real for me."
"Try to speak at a calm, steady ."
"I got my point without any trouble."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the sentences of the story in the correct order.

?When I forgot a word, I described it and kept going.
?A year ago, I translated every sentence in my head.
?Last week I spoke for ten minutes without translating -- that was my breakthrough.
?I stumbled on hard words and often felt tongue-tied.
?Then I stopped waiting for the perfect sentence.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

I can keep the conversation going, even when I forget a word.
What I mean is, I describe it in other words.
Give me a second to think, and then I will answer.
I made mistakes, but I got my point across.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the most natural thing to say in each real situation.

You forget the English word for something. What do you say?
A "Sorry, I forgot the word -- it is the thing you use to cook."
B "I stop now, goodbye."
C "Word no have me."
A friend asks about your weekend. You want to keep the conversation going. You say:
A "It was great -- I relaxed a lot. What about you?"
B "Good."
C "So do I."
Someone asks a hard question and you need a moment. You say:
A "That is a good question -- give me a second."
B "I think very fast now."
C "Repeat, please, again."
You want to add more detail to your idea. You say:
A "For example, last week it saved me a lot of time."
B "That is all, the end."
C "Neither do I."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

This is your final lesson -- look back on your English journey. How do you feel about speaking now, compared with a year ago? Talk for one or two minutes without stopping. If you forget a word, do not freeze: describe it, buy a second, and keep going. Try to use words from this lesson: fluency, confident, effortless, to keep up, breakthrough, to get across. Speak freely and enjoy it -- you have earned this.

Survival Card -- Lesson 20

1What I mean is...
2Let me think for a second.
3Can you tell me more about that?
4So, what about you?
5Anyway, as I was saying...

Complementary Activities

Extra material to practice outside class. Mark each one as done after you watch or listen.

Lesson 1 — Who Is Ana Paula? -- Introducing Yourself in Everyday English

Documentary
Lost World of the Maya (Full Episode) -- National Geographic

A beautiful documentary about the history and culture of the ancient Maya. It is real, everyday English about a subject you love -- history, culture and discovery. Connection to Lesson 1: notice how the narrator uses the present simple to describe facts and the present continuous for what the explorers are doing right now.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down three sentences: two in the present simple and one in the present continuous.

Watch on YouTube ↗
YouTube
Present Simple and Present Continuous -- 6 Minute Grammar (BBC Learning English)

A short and clear lesson on the exact grammar from today: the present simple for routines and facts, and the present continuous for what is happening now. Connection to Lesson 1: this is the difference between "I study English" and "Right now, I am studying".

Tip: pause after each example and say one sentence about your own day -- one with the present simple, one with the present continuous.

Watch on YouTube ↗
TED Talk
How to Stay Calm When You Know You Will Be Stressed -- Daniel Levitin (TED)

A calm, clear talk about health and the mind -- a wellness subject you enjoy, in careful, natural English. The speaker talks about his own routine and habits, so you hear the present simple in real use. Connection to Lesson 1: listen for how he describes what he usually does to stay healthy.

Tip: listen twice. Note five everyday phrases the speaker uses, and try to say them with clear pronunciation.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 2 — My Everyday Life -- Routines & Free Time

Documentary
Mental Health and Resilience -- The Secrets of Inner Strength (DW Documentary)

A calm, well-made documentary about wellbeing and healthy daily habits -- a subject you care about as a doctor. People describe how they rest, exercise and unwind to stay strong. Connection to Lesson 2: listen for how often people do things (they always, usually, sometimes, or rarely do them).

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down three sentences with an adverb of frequency about the habits in the film.

Watch on YouTube ↗
TED Talk
The Power of Habit -- Charles Duhigg (TEDx Talks)

A clear, friendly talk about how habits work and how small routines shape our day -- exactly the theme of this lesson. The speaker uses everyday examples in natural English. Connection to Lesson 2: notice the vocabulary of routine (habit, schedule) and the adverbs of frequency he uses.

Tip: pause after each story and describe one of your own habits, using "usually", "often" or "always".

Watch on YouTube ↗
YouTube
Adverbs of Frequency: How to Use Them and Where They Go -- English In A Minute (BBC Learning English)

A short, clear video on the exact grammar from today: how often, and where the adverb goes in the sentence. Connection to Lesson 2: this is the difference between "I always drink coffee" and "She is never late".

Tip: pause after each example and make one true sentence about your own week -- one before a main verb, one after the verb "to be".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 3 — My Neighborhood & My City

Documentary
Nature's Response to Urban Sprawl (DW Documentary)

A calm, beautiful documentary about how nature lives inside our cities -- parks, squares, avenues and the animals that share our streets. It is full of the exact vocabulary from this lesson. Connection to Lesson 3: listen for the places in a neighborhood (park, square, block, avenue) and for "there is / there are".

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write three sentences with "there is" or "there are" about places you see in the film.

Watch on YouTube ↗
TED Talk
The Walkable City -- Jeff Speck (TED)

A friendly, clear talk about what makes a neighborhood pleasant to live in: sidewalks, short blocks, shops on the corner and less traffic. The speaker describes cities in natural, everyday English. Connection to Lesson 3: notice the city words (avenue, block, sidewalk, downtown) and how he says where things are.

Tip: pause after each idea and describe your own neighborhood with a preposition of place (next to, across from, near).

Watch on YouTube ↗
YouTube
"There is" and "There are" -- Learn English Grammar (ABA English)

A short, clear video on the exact grammar from today: when to use "there is" and when to use "there are". Connection to Lesson 3: this is the difference between "There is a bakery" and "There are two parks".

Tip: pause after each example and make one true sentence about your street -- one with "there is", one with "there are".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 4 — Talking About the Past -- My Story

TED Talk
The Danger of a Single Story -- Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie (TED)

A famous storyteller talks about her own childhood and background, and how the stories we tell shape the way we see people. She narrates her life in clear, natural past simple. Connection to Lesson 4: listen for how she describes where she grew up and the turning points in her story.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down five past simple verbs she uses to tell her own story.

Watch on YouTube ↗
TED Talk
The Power of Believing That You Can Improve -- Carol Dweck (TED)

A short, inspiring talk about how people overcome challenges and achieve their goals through effort and belief -- exactly the spirit of your own journey as a doctor. Connection to Lesson 4: this is the language of challenge, to overcome, and to achieve, all words from today's lesson.

Tip: after watching, tell your own story of a challenge you overcame, in three or four past simple sentences.

Watch on YouTube ↗
TED Talk
Grit: The Power of Passion and Perseverance -- Angela Lee Duckworth (TED)

The speaker left one career to start a new one, and studied why some people never give up. It is a real story of a turning point and a new beginning, told in natural past simple. Connection to Lesson 4: notice the words career, turning point, and to achieve as she tells her story.

Tip: pause when she talks about her career change, and describe a milestone or turning point in your own life.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 5 — A Memorable Trip

Documentary
Portugal's Heartland -- Rick Steves' Europe

A calm, beautiful travel episode through small towns, the coast and breathtaking scenery. Rick Steves narrates his journey in clear, natural English. Connection to Lesson 5: listen for the travel words (journey, scenery, coast, landscape) and for stories told in the past.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write three sentences about the trip using "while I was watching" plus a past simple action.

Watch on YouTube ↗
TED Talk
The Value of Travel -- Rick Steves (TEDxRainier)

A warm, personal talk about why a memorable trip changes the way we see the world. The speaker tells travel stories in simple, everyday English. Connection to Lesson 5: notice how he moves between what happened (past simple) and what was happening around him (past continuous).

Tip: pause after each story and retell it in one sentence, using "while" plus "when".

Watch on YouTube ↗
YouTube
Past Simple and Past Continuous -- 6 Minute Grammar (BBC Learning English)

A short, clear video on the exact grammar from today: when to use the past simple and when to use the past continuous, with "when" and "while". Connection to Lesson 5: this is the difference between "I was walking" and "I saw".

Tip: pause after each example and make one true sentence about a trip -- one with the past continuous, one with the past simple.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 6 — Describing People & Places

TED Talk
Your Body Language May Shape Who You Are -- Amy Cuddy (TED)

A famous, warm talk about how we read and describe other people from the way they stand and move. She uses rich adjectives to describe confident and shy people. Connection to Lesson 6: listen for the words she uses to describe personality and presence.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down five adjectives she uses to describe people.

Watch on YouTube ↗
TED Talk
The Power of Vulnerability -- Brené Brown (TED)

A researcher describes the human qualities that make people warm, generous, and easy to connect with -- exactly the personality words from today. Connection to Lesson 6: she is a natural storyteller who describes people and their character in clear, everyday English.

Tip: after watching, describe one generous or easygoing person you know, in three or four sentences.

Watch on YouTube ↗
TED Talk
The Happy Secret to Better Work -- Shawn Achor (TED)

A funny, fast-talking speaker describes cheerful, positive people and the places that make us feel good. It is full of natural adjectives for personality and mood. Connection to Lesson 6: notice how he describes cheerful people and lively, positive environments.

Tip: pause and describe a cheerful person and a lively place in your own life, using at least four adjectives.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 7 — Comparing Things

Podcast
City Life vs Countryside Living -- Which Is Better? (Speak English With Nora)

A calm, slow-English podcast that compares living in the city and living in the countryside. The speaker weighs the advantages and drawbacks of each option in clear, natural language. Connection to Lesson 7: listen for comparatives and superlatives -- "more affordable", "quieter", "the best place to live".

Tip: pause after each point and repeat it as a comparison, using "more ... than" or "the most ...".

Listen on YouTube ↗
YouTube
Is It Better to Live in the City or Countryside? -- English on the Go! (Beginner)

A short, easy listening episode that compares the city and the countryside as two places to live. It is spoken slowly and repeats the key comparisons, so it is a friendly way to hear today's grammar in action. Connection to Lesson 7: every point is a comparison of two options -- exactly what you practiced.

Tip: after listening, choose two places you know and compare them out loud in three sentences.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Grammar Video
Comparatives and Superlatives -- 6 Minute Grammar (BBC Learning English)

A short, clear video on the exact grammar from today: how to build comparatives (cheaper, more modern) and superlatives (the cheapest, the most modern), including the irregular forms good/better/best. Connection to Lesson 7: this is the difference between "cheaper than" and "the cheapest".

Tip: pause after each rule and make one true sentence comparing two things you own or two places you know.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 8 — Midpoint Review -- How Far Has Ana Paula Come?

TED Talk
Grit: The Power of Passion and Perseverance -- Angela Lee Duckworth (TED)

A short, clear talk about why effort and perseverance matter more than talent when we learn something hard. It is full of the words from today: challenge, overcome, achieve, improve. Connection to Lesson 8: it is the perfect story about staying on a long journey and not giving up.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down three sentences she uses about effort and progress, then say your own version.

Watch on YouTube ↗
TEDx Talk
The First 20 Hours -- How to Learn Anything -- Josh Kaufman (TEDxCSU)

A friendly speaker explains how a small daily routine and the right habits help you improve fast at the start of any journey -- exactly like your English. Connection to Lesson 8: it uses simple, clear English about routines, habits, and early milestones.

Tip: pause and describe your own daily English routine in three or four sentences, using the present simple.

Watch on YouTube ↗
TED Talk
Try Something New for 30 Days -- Matt Cutts (TED)

A very short, easy talk about how a small new habit for thirty days can become an unforgettable turning point. It is slow, clear, and perfect for B1. Connection to Lesson 8: it is a real-life example of building a habit and reaching a personal milestone.

Tip: after watching, choose one new English habit for the second half of the course, and say why in one or two sentences.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 9 — Health & Wellbeing

Podcast
6 Minute English -- Wellbeing (BBC Learning English)

A calm, clear BBC compilation about wellbeing: how sleep, stress, and daily habits affect your health. Two hosts discuss real ideas slowly and explain the key words as they go. Connection to Lesson 9: listen for the health vocabulary -- wellbeing, stress, balanced, and how people give advice.

Tip: every time you hear a piece of advice, pause and repeat it with "You should ..." or "You must ...".

Listen on YouTube ↗
YouTube
Let's Talk About Healthy Habits -- English Listening Practice (B1)

A slow, friendly B1 listening episode about healthy habits: eating well, sleeping enough, staying active, and reducing stress. It is spoken clearly and repeats the key ideas, so it is a comfortable way to hear today's topic in action. Connection to Lesson 9: it is full of the habits you talked about with should and must.

Tip: after listening, name two healthy habits you should keep and one thing you must change.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Grammar Video
'Have To' and 'Must' -- 6 Minute Grammar (BBC Learning English)

A short, clear video on the exact grammar from today: how to use must for strong necessity, and how it compares with have to. It shows the base-verb pattern and gives simple, natural examples. Connection to Lesson 9: this is the difference between friendly advice (should) and a strong rule (must).

Tip: pause after each rule and make one true sentence about your own health with should or must.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 10 — Plans & Intentions

Grammar Video
Present Continuous and 'Going To' -- 6 Minute Grammar (BBC Learning English)

A short, clear video on the exact grammar from today: how to use "going to" for a plan you have already decided, and the present continuous for a fixed future arrangement. It gives simple, natural examples and a quick quiz. Connection to Lesson 10: this is the difference between an intention (I am going to) and an appointment in your diary (I am meeting).

Tip: pause after each example and make one true sentence about your own plans, one with "going to" and one with the present continuous.

Watch on YouTube ↗
YouTube
Talking About Future Plans in English -- B1 English Listening Practice

A slow, friendly B1 listening episode full of people talking about their future plans, goals, and intentions. It is spoken clearly and repeats the key structures, so it is a comfortable way to hear today's grammar in real conversation. Connection to Lesson 10: listen for "going to" and the present continuous every time someone talks about a plan.

Tip: every time you hear a future plan, pause and say your own version out loud with "I am going to ..." or "I am meeting ...".

Watch on YouTube ↗
Conversation
English Conversations for Personal Goals and Ambitions -- Listening Practice

A set of calm, natural conversations where people describe their personal goals and ambitions for the future. The speakers explain what they intend to do and what they are looking forward to, using clear, everyday language. Connection to Lesson 10: it is exactly the vocabulary of today -- goal, intend, look forward to, and plans for the year ahead.

Tip: after each conversation, write down one goal of your own and say what you are going to do to reach it.

Listen on YouTube ↗

Lesson 11 — Predictions & Promises

Podcast
6 Minute English -- Will robots take our jobs? (BBC Learning English)

Two hosts make predictions about the future of work: what jobs will change, what will probably disappear, and what will stay. The English is clear and the key words are explained as they go. Connection to Lesson 11: it is full of predictions with will, likely, and expect -- exactly today's grammar.

Tip: every time you hear a prediction, pause and repeat it as your own sentence with "I think it will ..." or "I doubt it will ...".

Listen on YouTube ↗
YouTube
How to Use Will Future for Predictions (OnScreen Academy)

A slow, friendly B1 lesson that shows exactly when and how to use will for predictions, with many simple examples about the weather, sport, and daily life. It repeats the base-verb pattern clearly. Connection to Lesson 11: it is the grammar you practiced today, explained step by step.

Tip: pause after each example and make one true prediction about your own week with will or won't.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Grammar Video
WILL / WON'T = Decision, Offer, Promise (Teachers Mark and Matt)

A short, clear video on the other side of today's grammar: using will for a decision you make right now, an offer to help, and a promise. It gives natural, everyday examples and simple explanations. Connection to Lesson 11: this is the "I'll help you" and "I promise I will" part of the lesson.

Tip: after the video, imagine a friend needs help and make three quick offers out loud, each starting with "I'll ...".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 12 — Experiences So Far

Grammar Video
The Present Perfect to Talk About Experiences (Speak Confident English)

A calm, clear lesson on exactly today's grammar: how to use the present perfect to talk about your life experiences, and how it is different from the past simple. The teacher speaks slowly and gives natural examples with "ever" and "never". Connection to Lesson 12: this is the difference between an experience (I have traveled) and a finished moment in the past.

Tip: pause after each example and make one true sentence about yourself, one with "I have..." and one with "I have never...".

Watch on YouTube ↗
Conversation
English Conversation About Life Experiences -- Present Perfect and Past Simple (English Panda)

A friendly, natural conversation where people catch up and talk about the experiences they have had, mixing the present perfect and the past simple. It is spoken clearly and repeats the key structures, so it is a comfortable way to hear today's grammar in real speech. Connection to Lesson 12: listen for "Have you ever...?" and "I have never..." every time someone talks about an experience.

Tip: every time you hear a life experience, pause and say your own version out loud with "I have..." or "I have never...".

Watch on YouTube ↗
Speaking Practice
Unusual Experiences -- Present Perfect and "Have You Ever" (Teacher Belen)

A short, fun speaking-practice video full of "Have you ever...?" questions about unusual experiences. Each question gives you a moment to answer out loud before moving on, so it is perfect for practicing the exact structure from today. Connection to Lesson 12: it is a mini version of the Quick Fire, letting you rehearse your own "I have..." and "I have never..." answers.

Tip: answer every question out loud in a full sentence, and add one extra detail, like where or when.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 13 — Midpoint Review -- How Far Has Ana Paula Come?

Grammar Video
Take a "Tenses Tour" -- 6 Minute Grammar (BBC Learning English)

A short, clear tour through the main English tenses in one place: present, past, present perfect and future. Two hosts explain, in slow and natural English, how to choose the right tense for the time you mean. Connection to Lesson 13: this is exactly today's review, the same tenses you mixed in your own story about your English journey.

Tip: each time they name a tense, pause and make one true sentence about your own English journey.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Grammar Overview
Verb Tenses: Present Simple, Present Continuous, Past Simple, Present Perfect and More (Sanook English)

A friendly overview that puts several tenses side by side with simple example sentences, so you can see how they are different and when to use each one. It is a comfortable way to check that you still remember every tense from the first half of the course. Connection to Lesson 13: it reviews the same forms you practiced today, all in one calm video.

Tip: after each tense, close your eyes and say one sentence about your routine, your past, or your goals.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Story
My First Journey Through New York City -- Learn English Through Story (English With Marco)

A gentle, spoken story about a first trip to a new city, told slowly and clearly. The narrator naturally mixes the past, the present and the future, exactly like you did today when you told your own journey. Connection to Lesson 13: listen to how one story moves between tenses without stopping, and notice the same review words, like journey, routine and highlight.

Tip: every time the tense changes, whisper which one it is (past, present, or future), then keep listening.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 14 — Phrasal Verbs in Real Life

Grammar Video
Top 10 Important Phrasal Verbs for Your Daily Routine (English with Emma, engVid)

A calm, clear lesson on exactly today's topic: ten common phrasal verbs you use for your daily routine, like wake up, get up and pick up. Emma explains each one slowly with simple examples on the board. Connection to Lesson 14: these are the same everyday verbs, and she shows how the small word (up, out, off) changes the meaning.

Tip: pause after each verb and make one true sentence about your own day, out loud.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Conversation
Talking about Daily Routines with Phrasal Verbs (Easy English)

Real people on the street describe their daily routines using natural phrasal verbs. It is spoken at a normal, friendly pace with English subtitles, so it is a comfortable way to hear today's verbs in real speech. Connection to Lesson 14: listen for get up, drop off, pick up and look after every time someone talks about a normal day.

Tip: every time you hear a phrasal verb, pause and say your own version out loud with "I ..." for your day.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Speaking Practice
Phrasal Verbs for Everyday Life: 32 Daily Routine Phrasal Verbs (JeN's Jyugyou)

A friendly, well-organized video that walks through a whole day, morning to night, with 32 daily-routine phrasal verbs and clear examples for each. It gives you a moment to repeat, so it is perfect for rehearsing the exact structures from today. Connection to Lesson 14: it is a longer version of your Quick Fire, so you can practice describing your own morning, work and evening.

Tip: choose five verbs from the video and use them to describe your real day out loud.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 15 — Making Your Voice Heard

Podcast
Keeping cars out of city centres -- 6 Minute English (BBC Learning English)

Two hosts discuss an everyday topic and share their opinions: should cars be banned from city centres? They agree, disagree, and give reasons in clear, natural English. Connection to Lesson 15: this is exactly the opinion language you practiced -- "in my opinion", "I see your point", and polite disagreement about a real issue.

Tip: every time a host gives an opinion, pause and say whether you agree or disagree, out loud, in one full sentence.

Listen on YouTube ↗
YouTube
Agreeing & Disagreeing in English -- Useful Expressions (Lingportal)

A clear, friendly B1 video that gives you a whole set of natural phrases to agree, disagree politely, and soften your words, with simple examples for each one. Connection to Lesson 15: it is a ready-made phrase bank for the exact function of today's lesson.

Tip: choose three new phrases from the video and use each one in a real conversation this week.

Watch on YouTube ↗
TED Talk
How to disagree productively and find common ground -- Julia Dhar (TED)

A short, inspiring talk on how to disagree with someone and still keep a good, respectful conversation, exactly the skill at the heart of today's lesson. The speaker talks slowly and clearly, and subtitles are available. Connection to Lesson 15: it shows why "I see your point, but ..." and finding common ground really matter.

Tip: watch with English subtitles, then write down one idea you agree with and one you would argue against.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 16 — Saying More With Less -- Ellipsis and Substitution in Everyday Conversation

Grammar Video
How to use "so do I" and "neither do I" (Perfect English Grammar)

A short, clear lesson on exactly today's grammar: how to agree with a short answer instead of repeating the whole sentence. The teacher shows how "so do I" answers a positive idea and "neither do I" answers a negative one, and how the verb changes with each tense. Connection to Lesson 16: this is the heart of ellipsis and substitution -- saying more with less.

Tip: after each example, pause and say your own true version out loud: "So do I" or "Neither do I."

Watch on YouTube ↗
Conversation
How to Keep Conversations Flowing in Natural English (BBC Learning English)

A friendly live class on how to keep a conversation going without awkward silences, using short reactions and follow-up questions. It is spoken at a natural, comfortable pace, so it is a great way to hear real English cohesion in action. Connection to Lesson 16: listen for short answers and reactions that let the speakers say less but stay connected, just like your "so", "one" and "neither".

Tip: every time you hear a short reaction, pause and repeat it out loud as if you were in the conversation.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Speaking Practice
English Conversation Practice: Greetings & Small Talk for A2-B1 Learners (Easy English Hub)

A calm, well-organized video that walks through natural greetings and small talk, with simple examples you can repeat. It gives you time to practice the exact casual openers you use to start and keep a chat going. Connection to Lesson 16: it is perfect for rehearsing today's conversation words -- small talk, chat, casual -- before you use short answers to keep the talk flowing.

Tip: choose three openers from the video and use them to start a real small-talk conversation out loud.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 17 — Numbers That Speak -- Talking About Data and Research Findings

Podcast
Making sense of the census -- 6 Minute English (BBC Learning English)

Two hosts talk about the census, the huge survey a country runs to count its people and collect data. They explain the figures in clear, natural English. Connection to Lesson 17: this is the exact language you practiced -- data, survey, percentage, figures, and trends -- used to talk about real numbers.

Tip: every time the hosts give a number, pause and say it out loud in a full sentence, using "roughly", "on average", or "the percentage of".

Listen on YouTube ↗
YouTube
Say & Write Numbers in English: percentages, fractions, decimals, large numbers (engVid)

A clear, friendly lesson that shows exactly how to read numbers aloud in English: percentages, fractions like "one in three", decimals like "two point five", and big figures. Connection to Lesson 17: it is a ready-made guide for the "read numbers aloud" part of today's Grammar Tip.

Tip: pause the video and read every number on the screen out loud before the teacher does; then check yourself.

Watch on YouTube ↗
TED Talk
The best stats you've ever seen -- Hans Rosling (TED)

A famous, energetic talk where a doctor and researcher makes data about health and the world come alive. He shows how numbers, averages, and trends tell a real human story. Connection to Lesson 17: it is a masterclass in describing data and trends clearly, in the same everyday, research-based English you are learning.

Tip: watch with English subtitles, then describe one trend from the talk in your own words, using "increased", "decreased", and "on average".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 18 — Navigating the Unexpected -- When You Don't Understand or Need Time

Conversation Lesson
How to Ask for Clarification in English -- How to Say You Don't Understand (Breathe English)

A clear, slow lesson with the exact phrases you use when you do not understand: how to say it politely, how to ask someone to repeat, and how to check what they mean. Connection to Lesson 18: this is the heart of today's repair strategies -- asking to clarify instead of pretending you understood.

Tip: after each phrase, pause the video and say it out loud, imagining a real phone call.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Speaking Practice
"Buying Time" Phrases for English Conversation (Fluency MC)

A short, rhythmic video full of natural phrases that give you a second to think before you answer -- exactly what you need when a question is hard or a word does not come. Connection to Lesson 18: these are your "buy time" tools, so you never freeze in the middle of a conversation.

Tip: choose two "buy time" phrases you like and use them the next time someone asks you a hard question.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Conversation
Conversation Fillers -- English Phrases to Avoid Awkward Silence (Let's Talk)

A friendly class on the small filler words and phrases that keep a conversation flowing while you think, so silence never feels awkward. It is spoken at a comfortable pace with plenty of examples. Connection to Lesson 18: it builds directly on today's word "filler" and shows how to use those little words to hold on and stay calm.

Tip: every time you hear a filler in the video, repeat it out loud, then use it in one sentence of your own.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 19 — The Language of Professional Identity -- Introducing Yourself as a Medical Professional in Transition

YouTube
Self introduction example at a job interview -- Practise Speaking English (mmmEnglish)

A clear, friendly lesson that models how to introduce yourself as a professional: your name, your field, your background, and your experience, in natural, confident English. Connection to Lesson 19: this is exactly the structure you practiced tonight -- who you are, what you have done, and where you are heading.

Tip: pause after the model introduction and record your own version out loud, using the present perfect for "how long" and the present simple for who you are now.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Podcast
Dreaming of being a chef -- 6 Minute English (BBC Learning English)

Two hosts talk about following a professional dream and what it really takes to build a career you love. They use clear, everyday English about work, training, and ambition. Connection to Lesson 19: it is the same language of professional identity and career choices you worked on tonight -- field, transition, and pursuing a goal.

Tip: whenever a host describes a career step, pause and say a sentence about your own path using "I have worked", "I studied", or "I am pursuing".

Listen on YouTube ↗
TED Talk
Why Some of Us Don't Have One True Calling -- Emilie Wapnick (TED)

A warm, encouraging talk about people who follow many paths and reinvent their careers, and why changing direction is a strength, not a weakness. Connection to Lesson 19: it speaks directly to a professional in transition, celebrating adaptability and the value of everything you have already learned.

Tip: watch with English subtitles, then describe your own transition in three sentences, using "I studied", "I have worked", and "right now, I am".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 20 — Fluency Under Pressure -- Sustained Conversation and Spontaneous Production

Fluency Lesson
How to Stop Translating in Your Head: Speak English Fluently (Speak English With Vanessa)

A warm, clear lesson on the exact habit you have just left behind -- translating every sentence before you speak -- and how to think and talk directly in English instead. Connection to Lesson 20: this is the heart of today's milestone, speaking spontaneously without a script in your head.

Tip: after each tip, pause and describe your day out loud in English, without translating a single sentence.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Conversation
How to Keep a Conversation Going in English (Eat Sleep Dream English)

A friendly, natural class packed with real phrases that keep a conversation flowing -- reacting, passing the turn, and asking follow-up questions -- so silence never takes over. Connection to Lesson 20: it builds directly on today's fluency toolkit, especially passing the turn and elaborating.

Tip: pick three phrases you like and try them the next time you chat -- react, add a detail, then ask "what about you?".

Watch on YouTube ↗
Speaking Strategy
Top 5 Tips for When You Get Stuck or Forget a Word in English Conversation (Advanced English Communication)

Five practical strategies for the exact moment a word disappears from your head -- describe it, speak in general terms, or paraphrase -- so you never freeze or switch to your first language. Connection to Lesson 20: this is today's "paraphrase a missing word" move, the skill that keeps you fluent under pressure.

Tip: choose one object at home, forget its name on purpose, and describe it in three different ways out loud.

Watch on YouTube ↗
